summary: Pseudo peer review: 12 independent critical reviewers each read the de-comma'd individual-guidance study from a distinct lens and return severity-ranked findings, including out-of-scope flags

reviews.lens: Pāli philology — term accuracy, glosses, etymology, diacritics, word-sense claims
reviews.overall: The study is, on the whole, philologically careful: diacritics are clean throughout (the seven ariya-puggala terms, the carita compounds, vitakka/vicāra/pīti/sukha, samādhismiṃ mattaso kārī, vossaggārammaṇaṃ, na sārato paccetabbaṃ are all correctly typed), and the three most checkable load-bearing readings hold against the live corpus: CND 19 does carry the six-temperament list (rāgacarita / dosacarita / mohacarita / vitakkacarita / saddhācarita / ñāṇacarita) with the asubha→greed, mettā→hate matching the paper describes; PS 3.5 does list the eight cariyā (iriyāpatha, āyatana, sati, samādhi, ñāṇa, magga, patti, lokattha) in the older conduct-sense; and the seven-person roster glosses are sound. The sense-audit discipline (pruning anusaya/cetopariya/paññāvimutti as doctrinal-list noise, the homograph cautions in G3) is genuinely good lexical hygiene. My biggest concern is the single most interpretively loaded gloss in the paper — appamāṇa read as "a refusal of measure rather than a claim of the infinite." That gloss is doing heavy thematic work (it is the keystone of the world-domain half of the function-to-essence thesis) and it is the one place where the lexicon actively cuts against the reading: in the canonical kasiṇa formula appamāṇa is the positive pole opposed to paritta (limited), i.e. an assertion of boundlessness, and appamāṇa/appamaññā is the standard word for the boundless brahmavihāras. The carita/cariyā "one word changing its job" framing is also looser than the morphology warrants. These are fixable with hedging; none overturns the headline counts.

findings.issue: The keystone cosmology gloss reads appamāṇa as 'a refusal of measure rather than a claim of the infinite.' This is lexically contestable in the very place it matters. In the canonical kasiṇa material (e.g. the paritta/appamāṇa kasiṇa contrast, MN 127, AN 10.29) appamāṇa is the POSITIVE pole opposed to paritta 'limited' — i.e. it positively asserts that the meditation sign is extended without bound, not that the texts decline to quantify. appamāṇa is likewise the standard term for the boundless brahmavihāras (appamaññā). So 'measureless = a refusal of measure, NOT a claim of the infinite' inverts the term's ordinary force to make it fit the function-vs-essence frame. The paper itself wobbles, glossing the same canonical state as both 'measureless' and 'hyperbolic' (abstract) — those are different claims.
findings.where: Abstract: 'the kasiṇa that the canon offers as a measureless perception (*appamāṇa*, a refusal of measure rather than a claim of the infinite) becomes a manufactured disc'; repeated in §VII 'as a measureless perception (*appamāṇa*) becomes a manufactured disc'
findings.recommendation: Drop or heavily hedge the 'rather than a claim of the infinite' clause. The defensible claim is the layer-count one (appamāṇa-kasiṇa in the canon → physical disc in the commentary); the interpretive claim that canonical appamāṇa means 'declines to measure' rather than 'positively boundless' should be stated as a reading, with the paritta/appamāṇa opposition acknowledged, since that opposition makes appamāṇa look like an assertion of unboundedness.

findings.category: word-sense / canon's own use of 'the infinite'
findings.issue: The world-domain argument repeatedly contrasts a canon that leaves things 'measureless or hyperbolic' with a commentary that 'counts' and 'bounds.' But the canon itself names an explicitly INFINITE object in its own meditative cosmology: ākāsānañcāyatana, the base of the infinitude of space (anantaṃ ākāsaṃ), and viññāṇānañcāyatana. The thesis 'open/measureless canon → bounded/measured commentary' is complicated by the canon's own ananta-formations. The prompt's ananta/appamāṇa pair is relevant here: the paper uses appamāṇa to mean 'declines the infinite' while the canon elsewhere affirms ananta of space.
findings.where: Abstract and §VII: 'What was measureless or hyperbolic is later counted in *yojana*s'; 'closes an open cosmos into a measured one'
findings.recommendation: Acknowledge the formless attainments' anantaṃ ākāsaṃ as a canonical case where the canon DOES assert the infinite, so the 'open canon vs measured commentary' contrast is a tendency in the cosmographic register (cakkavāḷa, yojana, kappa) and not a blanket canonical reticence about the infinite.

findings.issue: The hinge argument frames carita and cariyā as 'a single word' that can be 'watched changing its job,' with the temperament sense a 're-coinage in progress beside the older conduct sense.' Morphologically these are two distinct derivatives of √car: carita is the (substantivized) past participle 'conduct/behaviour,' cariyā the action-noun 'conduct/practice/way of living.' They are cognate, not one word. More substantively, the temperament sense of carita is a SPECIALIZATION of its persistent core sense ('the conduct one habitually engages in' → 'character'), not a fresh coinage; sucarita/duccarita ('good/bad conduct') keep that core sense live throughout. Calling it a 're-coinage' overstates the semantic discontinuity.
findings.where: §IV: 'a single word can be watched changing its job'; 'The temperament sense is caught here mid-fork, a re-coinage in progress beside the older conduct sense.'
findings.recommendation: Soften to 'two cognate derivatives of √car' and frame the temperament sense as a specialization/narrowing of the conduct sense rather than a 're-coinage.' The dating evidence (PS 3.5 conduct-sense co-present with CND 19 temperament-sense at the same stratum) survives this softening; the claim that needs trimming is the lexical-discontinuity rhetoric.

findings.issue: kāyasakkhi is glossed '(who has touched the deep absorptions)' and elsewhere 'who has touched the deep absorptions' / 'touched the eight liberations with the body.' The canonical definition (e.g. MN 70, AN 9.43-45) is specifically one who has touched the eight VIMOKKHAS (aṭṭha vimokkhā) with the body AND has some taints destroyed by wisdom. 'Deep absorptions' blurs the eight liberations (which run through the formless and to saññāvedayitanirodha) into the jhānas. The paper gets it right in §VII ('touched the eight liberations with the body') but loose in §II ('who has touched the deep absorptions').
findings.where: §II: 'the body-witness (*kāyasakkhi*, who has touched the deep absorptions)'
findings.recommendation: Make §II consistent with the later, accurate phrasing: kāyasakkhi = one who has touched the eight liberations (aṭṭha vimokkha) with the body, not merely 'the deep absorptions.'

findings.issue: ubhatobhāgavimutta is glossed 'through the formless attainments and wisdom together.' The 'both portions' (ubhato-bhāga) are conventionally the liberation of mind through the peaceful/formless liberations (the 'rūpa'/arūpa kāya side) AND liberation by wisdom (the destruction of taints). 'Formless attainments and wisdom together' is a fair shorthand, but 'formless attainments' slightly narrows the first portion, which in the standard account is the eight vimokkhas (the same set the kāyasakkhi touches), of which the formless are a part.
findings.where: §II: 'the one freed both ways (*ubhatobhāgavimutta*, through the formless attainments and wisdom together)'
findings.recommendation: Optionally widen 'the formless attainments' to 'the peaceful (formless) liberations' or 'the eight liberations' to align with the kāyasakkhi gloss and the standard commentarial reading; minor and defensible as-is.

findings.issue: Nibbāna is glossed as defined 'only in the negative, the unmade and the unborn.' The locus classicus (Ud 8.3) is a four-term formula: ajāta (unborn), abhūta (unbecome), akata (unmade), asaṅkhata (unconditioned). 'The unmade and the unborn' cites two of four and silently drops abhūta/asaṅkhata. Not wrong, but a reader checking the Pāli will find the formula is a tetrad.
findings.where: §VII: 'the canon defines nibbāna only in the negative, the unmade and the unborn'
findings.recommendation: Either name the full tetrad (ajāta abhūta akata asaṅkhata) or signal 'e.g.' so the two-term citation is not read as the whole formula. Also note 'only in the negative' overstates: the canon also uses positive epithets (santa, paṇīta, amata, etc.).

findings.issue: G1's pruned false positives for the sabhāva count are named as 'purisabhāva' and 'ekaṃsa-bhāvita.' purisabhāva (purisa+bhāva, 'manhood/male state') is a correct false-positive for a %sabhāva% substring search. But 'ekaṃsa-bhāvita' is an odd pairing: it would only collide with a sabhāva substring via sandhi/hyphenation (…sa-bhāvita), and bhāvita ('developed') is a different lexeme from bhāva. Naming it as a representative false positive is either a typo for a different form or an imprecise example.
findings.where: G1: 'once the unrelated compounds *purisabhāva* and *ekaṃsa-bhāvita* are set aside'; cf. DISCOVERY-PASS '*purisabhāva* / *ekaṃsa-bhāvita* false positives'
findings.recommendation: Verify the actual canonical %sabhāva% raw hits and cite the real false-positive forms; if ekaṃsa-bhāvita is not literally among them, replace it. The 'near zero canonical floor' conclusion is unaffected, but the example should be a real row.

findings.issue: The claim that canonical upacāra means 'a vicinity or an approach: the outskirts of a village, the near company of women' is correct for the attested senses, but 'the near company of women' is a narrow and slightly tendentious gloss of gāmūpacāra/itthi-upacāra-type usage. upacāra in the canon also routinely means 'access/approach (to a place), service, attendance, conduct toward.' Citing only 'outskirts of a village' and 'near company of women' undersamples the canonical range and reads as cherry-picked to maximize distance from 'access concentration.'
findings.where: §VII (concentration section): 'there it means a vicinity or an approach: the outskirts of a village, the near company of women.'
findings.recommendation: Broaden the canonical-sense examples (approach/vicinity/attendance) or add 'among other senses,' so the contrast with the commentarial technical sense rests on the absence of the meditative sense rather than on a curated pair of glosses.

findings.issue: vitakka is glossed 'applied thought' in §VII but the antidote in §II is 'mindfulness of breathing ... to cut off thinking' and CND 19 assigns ānāpānassati to the vitakkacarita (the 'speculation/discursive-thought temperament'). The paper never names the sixth canonical temperament-pair explicitly: CND 19's six are rāga/dosa/moha/vitakka/saddhā/ñāṇa, whereas the COMMENTARIAL standard six are rāga/dosa/moha/saddhā/buddhi/vitakka. The vitakka- and ñāṇa/buddhi-cells differ in labeling across strata; the paper's '...and so through the six' glosses over that the canonical (CND) sixth set is not identical to the Visuddhimagga's.
findings.where: §IV: 'and so through the six (CND 19)'; the six are rāga/dosa/moha/vitakka/saddhā/ñāṇa in CND 19
findings.recommendation: Consider noting that the CND 19 sextet (…vitakka…ñāṇa) and the Visuddhimagga sextet (…buddhi…vitakka) are not term-identical, which actually strengthens the 'minted at the hinge, then reworked' thesis rather than weakening it.

findings.issue: samādhismiṃ mattaso kārī is rendered 'doers "to a measure"' / 'a doer "to a measure."' mattaso is 'in moderation / to a moderate/partial degree,' and the standard contrast is paripūrakārī (one who fulfils completely) vs mattaso kārī (one who does only partially). 'To a measure' is an acceptable literal rendering but ambiguous in English (it can read as 'to some standard'); the intended sense is 'only partially / to a limited degree.'
findings.where: §VII: 'are only doers "to a measure" in concentration and wisdom (*samādhismiṃ mattaso kārī*)'
findings.recommendation: Gloss mattaso as 'to a moderate/partial degree' at first use so 'to a measure' is not misread; the paripūrakārī/mattaso-kārī polarity is the load-bearing contrast and should be unambiguous.

findings.issue: gotrabhū is glossed 'the change-of-lineage moment.' This is the conventional rendering, but gotrabhū is contested: literally 'one in whom the [noble] lineage comes to be / become-of-the-lineage,' and in the Abhidhamma it names a specific citta, not merely a 'moment.' Calling it 'the change-of-lineage moment' imports the later technical (Abhidhammic) reading into the term itself, which slightly prejudges the very canon-vs-Abhidhamma point the sentence is making.
findings.where: §VII: 'The change-of-lineage moment (*gotrabhū*) is barely present in the suttas and becomes a fixture of the Abhidhamma's analysis of the mind'
findings.recommendation: Minor: 'change-of-lineage' alone (without 'moment,' which is the Abhidhammic citta-framing) would keep the gloss neutral between strata.

findings.issue: The corpus snippets use ṁ (anusvāra with overdot, CST/VRI style: Dvāhaṁ, asubhakathaṁ) while the paper consistently normalizes to ṃ (dot-under: savitakkaṃ, appamāṇaṃ, samādhismiṃ). Both are valid niggahīta conventions and the paper is internally consistent (ṃ throughout), but a reader cross-checking quoted Pāli against the live CST rows will see ṁ vs ṃ. Not an error, just a normalization the method note does not mention.
findings.where: Throughout, e.g. §VII 'savitakkaṃ savicāraṃ vivekajaṃ pītisukhaṃ' (paper) vs corpus 'asubhakathaṁ' (CST ṁ)
findings.recommendation: Optionally add one line to the method note stating niggahīta is normalized to ṃ in citations, since the underlying CST edition uses ṁ.

reviews.lens: Textual stratigraphy and relative dating (the six-stratum scheme and the "four jumps")
reviews.overall: The study's core stratigraphic intuition is sound and, on its single hardest datum (zero temperament-carita compounds in the four Nikayas and the seven Abhidhamma books, reconfirmed live), genuinely defensible: that is a hard lexical absence no register label is doing work in. The function-vs-essence axis is a real and well-chosen cut. But the six-stratum scheme that carries the "four jumps" headline rests on a work-slug bucketing that I was able to break in three concrete ways against the live corpus. (1) The corpus double-ingests the para-canonical works: the Niddesa, Netti, and Petakopadesa each exist twice under different slugs (CST copy in pli-kn as kn15/kn16/kn19; SuttaCentral copy as pli-nd/pli-ne/pli-pe), and the temperament compound is present in BOTH copies, so the "para/late" mass that anchors the central jump is inflated and the published distribution does not cleanly reconcile with the live counts. (2) The same term is coded "late-canonical" in the count harness (HARDENING-CENSUS puts nd/ps in 2late) but "para-canonical" in the prose (the abstract, the section-IV title, FINDINGS-v2), so the load-bearing hinge stratum is named two incompatible things. (3) The pli-kn slug is a Khuddaka grab-bag that mixes the earliest Khuddaka (Dhammapada, Udana, Itivuttaka) and the latest (Niddesa, Netti) under one work_slug, so any count that strata-codes by slug treats kn as a single bucket while the prose treats its parts as different strata. None of these overturns the headline absence, but they directly weaken the precision of the "four distinct jumps" claim and the published per-work distribution. The biggest concern: several "jumps" are partly artifacts of how works were bucketed and double-counted, and the paper presents the jump-localization with more confidence than the bucketing supports.

findings.issue: The corpus double-ingests the para-canonical works under two parallel slug systems, and the temperament compound is present in BOTH copies. The Niddesa exists as pli-nd (cnd* ids, the paper's own anchor cnd19) AND as pli-kn kn15/kn16 (Mahaniddesa/Culaniddesa, cst-s0501m ids); the Nettippakarana exists as pli-ne AND as pli-kn kn19; the Petakopadesa as pli-pe (and a kn copy). Live counts of the temperament compound: pli-nd=3, pli-ne=3, pli-pe=3, pli-mil=2 in the SuttaCentral copies, PLUS kn15=2, kn16=1, kn19=3 in the CST copy. The same Culaniddesa matrix is counted in two different work_slugs sitting in (nominally) the same stratum. The 'para-canonical/late' mass that anchors the central temperament jump is therefore inflated by duplication, and the headline distribution ('thirty Visuddhimagga, ten in the analytical Khuddaka', '53 mula rows') cannot be reproduced cleanly: the live 6-defilement compound returns 28 in pli-vism (not 30) and 9 in pli-kn for the mula subset.
findings.where: Section V: 'of the fifty-three structurally-canonical rows that hold the disambiguated temperament compound, thirty are the Visuddhimagga' (and FINDINGS-v2 'thirty Visuddhimagga, ten in the analytical Khuddaka, ... three Niddesa ... one Patisambhidamagga')
findings.recommendation: De-duplicate before counting: pick ONE ingest of each para-canonical work (resolve pli-nd vs pli-kn-kn15/16, pli-ne vs pli-kn-kn19) and recompute every per-work and per-stratum count, or state explicitly that counts are taken from one named copy and that the duplicate is excluded. Reconcile the published 30/53 to the live 28 (state whether nanacarita or a stem broader than the six defilement compounds is included to reach 30). Until de-duplicated, do not present per-work row tallies as exact.

findings.issue: The same hinge stratum is labelled two incompatible things across the study's own documents. The count harness (HARDENING-CENSUS 'The fine stratum code') places nd (Niddesa) and ps (Patisambhidamagga) in stratum 2late = late-canonical, and reserves stratum 4para = para-canonical for Netti/Petakopadesa/Milinda only. But the readable paper's abstract, the section-IV title ('In the para-canonical Khuddaka'), and FINDINGS-v2 repeatedly call the Culaniddesa (a Niddesa text) 'para-canonical'. So the load-bearing hinge work is 'late-canonical' in the harness that produces the jump counts and 'para-canonical' in the prose that interprets them. This matters because the 'four jumps' headline distinguishes a 'late-canonical -> commentary' jump (jump 2) from a 'canon -> commentary' jump (jump 3); if nd/ps are mis-stratified relative to ne/pe/mil, the assignment of the carita jump to one bucket versus another is not stable.
findings.where: Abstract/Section IV 'In the para-canonical Khuddaka: a word changes its job' vs HARDENING-CENSUS '2late (late-canonical): ap, bv, cp, pv, vv, nd (Niddesa), ps (Patisambhidamagga), ja, kn' and '4para (para-canonical): ne (Netti), pe (Petakopadesa), mil (Milinda)'
findings.recommendation: Fix the terminology once and propagate. Decide whether the Niddesa/Patisambhidamagga are 'late-canonical Khuddaka' (defensible: they are in the Khuddaka Nikaya) or 'para-canonical' (defensible only loosely), and use that label consistently in the prose AND the harness. Note that calling a canonical Khuddaka text 'para-canonical' is itself a philological overstatement: the Niddesa and Patisambhidamagga are canonical Khuddaka, not para-canonical in the technical sense reserved for Netti/Petakopadesa/Milinda.

findings.issue: The pli-kn work_slug is a Khuddaka catch-all spanning kn1 through kn19 in one bucket: it holds the EARLIEST Khuddaka (kn1 Khuddakapatha, kn2 Dhammapada, kn3 Udana, kn4 Itivuttaka) and the LATEST (kn15 Mahaniddesa, kn16 Culaniddesa, kn19 Nettippakarana) under a single slug. Any harness that strata-codes by work_slug (run_samadhi.py lumps everything non-Nikaya/non-comm/non-tika into one '2para' layer; HARDENING-CENSUS lists 'kn' as a single 2late entry) cannot separate early-Khuddaka from late-Khuddaka content inside pli-kn. The study's prose, by contrast, treats kn2/kn3 (Dhammapada/Udana) as early-canonical and kn16 (Culaniddesa) as the late hinge. The stratum a given Khuddaka row gets therefore depends on which harness ran, and the two harnesses disagree.
findings.where: HARDENING-CENSUS '1early ... + early Khuddaka (dhp, ud, iti, snp, thag, thig, kp)' and '2late ... ja, kn'; run_samadhi.py LAYER_CASE collapsing all non-canon/non-comm into '2para'. Live: pli-kn contains kn1 'Saranattayam', kn2 'Dhammapadapali', kn3 'Udanapali' AND kn15 'Mahaniddesapali', kn16 'Culaniddesapali', kn19 'Nettippakaranapali'.
findings.recommendation: Split pli-kn by sub-book (the substring(id from 'mul-(kn[0-9]+)') key already works) before strata-coding, and map each kn-number to its correct stratum (kn1-7 early; kn10-14 mostly late verse; kn15-16 Niddesa late; kn17-18 Patisambhidamagga; kn19 Netti). State which kn sub-books fall in which stratum explicitly. Flag that the early-Khuddaka separate slugs (pli-dhp, pli-ud, pli-iti...) ALSO duplicate content that sits inside pli-kn, so kn-based early counts risk double-counting too.

findings.issue: The 'early -> Abhidhamma' jumps (jhananga, gotrabhu, ekaggata) treat the seven Abhidhamma books as one homogeneous late-ish stratum, which buries two confounds. First, the gotrabhu '6 early -> 210 Abhidhamma' jump: live, gotrabhu is 197 in pli-abhidhamma but the developed gotrabhu-nana ALSO sits in the Patisambhidamagga (pli-ps, late-canonical Khuddaka, 3+ rows) and pli-kn, so the term's conceptual hardening is shared between Abhidhamma and late Khuddaka, not localized to 'the Abhidhamma'. The 197 is overwhelmingly the momentary-citta Patthana/Yamaka analysis, i.e. one genre inside the Abhidhamma. Second, the seven Abhidhamma books are not a single chronological stratum on the consensus dating the study otherwise leans on (the Kathavatthu and parts of the Patthana are markedly later than the Dhammasangani/Vibhanga). Coding them as one '3abh' bucket lets a late-Abhidhamma term read as an early-Abhidhamma jump.
findings.where: Section VII 'It appears first in the seven Abhidhamma books'; HARDENING-CENSUS 'change-of-lineage citta (gotrabhu) | 6 | 4 | 210 | ... early->Abhidhamma'
findings.recommendation: Either acknowledge in the Limitations that the seven Abhidhamma books are treated as one stratum and that intra-Abhidhamma chronology (Kathavatthu/Patthana late) is not resolved, OR sub-stratify the Abhidhamma. For gotrabhu specifically, note that the Patisambhidamagga carries the developed gotrabhu-nana, so the jump is 'early canon -> {Abhidhamma + late Khuddaka}', not Abhidhamma alone.

findings.issue: FINDINGS-v2 explicitly claims the stratum codes are 'not at risk of the genre-as-date circularity ... because the decisive evidence is ... a hard lexical absence'. That defence holds for the ONE absence datum (carita = 0 in Nikayas/Abhidhamma) but the readable paper extends the same stratum scheme to ~two dozen positive-count transitions (sabhava, ajjhasaya, upacara, cakkavala, kappavinasa, jhananga...) whose 'jump' verdicts depend entirely on the relative ordering of works the study assigns by genre/consensus, not by an absence. For those, the genre-as-date risk the paper disavows IS live: e.g. ajjhasaya '4 canon -> 701 attha' reads as a sutta->commentary jump only because the commentary is dated later, which is the very ordering at issue. The Limitations section concedes 'relative order, not calendar date' but does not concede that for the positive-count jumps the ordering is the genre/consensus assumption, not an independent measurement.
findings.where: Section VIII 'The dating is relative order, not calendar date; the one firm exception is a hard count: the absence of the temperament compound'
findings.recommendation: Generalize the honesty guard: state that for every positive-count transition (as opposed to the single carita absence) the jump verdict inherits the consensus stratum ordering and is therefore as secure as that ordering, no more. The current text gives the absence's firmness as if it covered the whole 'four jumps' result.

findings.category: recall artifact admitted but not propagated to the headline
findings.issue: HARDENING-CENSUS notes that the present-root-sort 'early=0 is an artifact' (the canonical refrain inserts va: 'saragam va cittam', so the contiguous-match misses it) and that the heart-blood physiology is 'a single vivid passage, not a distributed countable term'. These recall caveats materially affect two of the strays/jumps, but the readable paper presents the present-root preservation and the sub-commentary physiology jump without carrying the caveat that one is a match-artifact zero and the other is an n=1 passage, not a count. A reader of FINDINGS-readable cannot tell that the 'Abhidhamma carries the present-root sort verbatim' stray rests on a refrain the harness could not contiguous-match.
findings.where: Section III 'The Yamaka carries the present-root sort straight from the discourses' and Section VI 'Six heart-blood colours, keyed to the six temperaments'; cf HARDENING-CENSUS 'Recall caveats: saragam cittam early=0 is an artifact ... Heart-blood physiology is a single vivid ... passage, not a distributed countable term'
findings.recommendation: Carry both caveats into the readable paper: flag the present-root 'preservation' as resting on a refrain whose early count is suppressed by the va-insertion (so it is preservation, not measured by a clean count), and flag the heart-blood physiology as a single passage (n=1), not a counted distribution, so it is not weighed as a 'jump' on the same footing as the term-counted ones.

findings.issue: The Khuddaka split codes ja (Jataka) as '2late' (late-canonical). The Jataka is a special case: the canonical gathas (verses) are old, while the prose framing (the bulk of pli-ja's 547 rows, the atthakatha-origin narrative) is commentarial-era. Bucketing all of ja as one 'late-canonical' stratum mixes genuinely old verse with much-later prose, paralleling the pli-kn grab-bag problem. None of the headline carita/samadhi terms land in ja, so this does not move the central result, but it is a latent stratum-coding defect if the harness is reused for a term that does occur in Jataka prose.
findings.where: HARDENING-CENSUS '2late (late-canonical): ap, bv, cp, pv, vv, nd (Niddesa), ps (Patisambhidamagga), ja, kn'
findings.recommendation: Note that the Jataka verse/prose split is unresolved by the slug-level coding and that ja-2late is a convenience bucket; if a future term hits Jataka prose, separate gatha from the commentarial frame.

findings.issue: Coding the Patisambhidamagga as late-canonical (2late) is defensible and is the consensus view, but the paper leans on it asymmetrically: the progress-of-insight nana ladder is credited as a 'late(Patis)->commentary' seedbed jump (HARDENING-CENSUS row), while in the same breath the Patisambhidamagga's eight cariya are used as evidence of the OLD conduct-sense being 'alive a few pages away' from the new temperament-sense. The Patisambhidamagga thus functions simultaneously as the late-canonical innovator (insight ladder) and the conservative retainer (old cariya) depending on which point is being made. That is not necessarily wrong, but the dual role should be made explicit so it does not read as cherry-picking the Patisambhidamagga's stratum to suit each claim.
findings.where: Section IV 'the Patisambhidamagga, sibling to the Niddesa, uses cariya to mean exactly the older thing' vs HARDENING-CENSUS 'progress-of-insight ladder ... late(Patis)->commentary | support'
findings.recommendation: State plainly that the Patisambhidamagga is internally mixed (it both retains the old cariya sense and seeds the commentarial insight-ladder vocabulary), and that this is itself evidence the 'jump' is a gradient inside a single work, not a clean inter-stratum step.

findings.issue: The hardening census reports '~63% clean support, 70% if mixed counts' of ~27 transitions, and concludes H1 is 'substantially supported but not universal'. A 63% support rate with 8 strays and 2 mixed is a fairly weak base for the readable paper's confident 'It happens at four separate jumps'. The readable paper foregrounds the four-jump result and relegates the ~37% non-supporting share to 'a clear minority run against the trend, and they are not few'. The phrase 'and they are not few' is honest, but the headline framing still leads with the multi-jump claim as if it were the dominant finding rather than a ~60% tendency.
findings.where: Section VII 'It happens at four separate jumps' vs HARDENING-CENSUS 'H1 is substantially supported but not universal, and H0 is partly true' / '63% clean support'
findings.recommendation: Surface the support fraction (roughly three in five transitions localize to a single jump; the rest are early-system, preservation, regression, or inverse) in the readable paper's 'Where the hardening happens' section, so the four-jump claim is calibrated to its ~60% base rate rather than stated as a clean result.

reviews.lens: Buddhist-doctrine critic — doctrinal accuracy and even-handedness of the canonical/commentarial claims
reviews.overall: The study's central thesis — that an adaptive, function-and-route teaching is progressively hardened into fixed-type and fixed-measure systems across the textual strata — is doctrinally well-founded and, on its load-bearing philological claims (the zero carita-compound count in the four Nikayas and Abhidhamma, the Culaniddesa hinge, the Visuddhimagga's na sarato paccetabbam disclaimer, the seven ariya-puggala roster, the yuganaddha/vehicle-split contrast, the upacara/appana/khanika absence from the canon, the samadhismim mattaso kari gradient), it holds up against the corpus. AN 9.36's "platform through the base of nothingness," AN 3.86's graded-trainings formula, AN 4.170's four routes, and the MN 22 raft are all cited correctly. The serious problem is the handling of the eight liberations (attha vimokkha). The study repeatedly glosses them as "the deep absorptions through the formless states" / "the peaceful formless states transcending form," which is doctrinally wrong: the canonical eight (DN 15) comprise three FORM-based (rupa) liberations, the four formless attainments, and the cessation of perception-and-feeling. Worse, the text the study leans on — MN 70 and SN 12.70 — actually splits the persons by the FORMLESS liberations (santa vimokkha atikkamma rupe aruppa), not by "the eight liberations," while the standard canonical definition of the kayasakkhi (body-witness) uses the full eight (incl. form-based). The study fuses two distinct technical terms and so misstates what the seven-noble-persons split actually turns on. Separately, "the canon defines nibbana only in the negative" is one-sided — the Asankhata Samyutta (SN 43) supplies a substantial positive epithet-set (siva, khema, suddhi, tana, dipa, parayana). These are fixable within the study and do not sink the thesis, but they are real doctrinal errors that a specialist reviewer would catch immediately. A structural concern: the readable version's cosmology and nibbana claims appear to extend well beyond what the rigor-track FINDINGS-v2.md actually coded, so they carry less evidentiary backing than the carita core.

findings.issue: The eight liberations (attha vimokkha) are repeatedly glossed as if they were the formless attainments. But the canonical list (DN 15, verbatim: 'Rupi rupani passati ayam pathamo vimokkho ... Subhanteva adhimutto hoti ayam tatiyo vimokkho' then the four aruppa then cessation) comprises THREE form-based (rupa) liberations, the four formless bases, AND the cessation of perception-and-feeling — not 'the deep absorptions through the formless states.' Equating attha vimokkha with the aruppa is a category error: it drops the three rupa liberations and the nirodha attainment, and it conflates 'liberations' with 'absorptions.'
findings.where: Sec VII (line 85): 'does not dwell having touched the eight liberations, the peaceful formless states transcending form, with the body'; and Sec VII (line 71): 'without the eight liberations (*attha vimokkha*, the deep absorptions through the formless states) that the body-witness has touched'
findings.recommendation: Either define attha vimokkha correctly (three form-based + four formless + cessation, per DN 15) and keep the term, OR — since the argument actually only needs the formless attainments — drop the words 'eight liberations' here and cite the formless liberations directly. Do not gloss the eight as 'the formless states' or 'the deep absorptions.'

findings.category: doctrinal accuracy — seven noble persons split
findings.issue: The study says the both-ways-freed/body-witness split turns on 'the eight liberations,' but MN 70 (verbatim) defines the ubhatobhagavimutta by 'ye te santa vimokkha atikkamma rupe aruppa te kayena phusitvā viharati' — the FORMLESS liberations (aruppa), not 'the eight liberations.' Conversely, the standard canonical definition of the kayasakkhi (e.g. Puggalapannatti) uses the full EIGHT liberations (attha vimokkhe kayena phusitva), which include the three form-based ones. The study fuses these two distinct technical formulations and so misstates what the roster split actually cleaves on. SN 12.70 (Susima), the study's own anchor, also reads 'santa vimokkha atikkamma rupe aruppa' — formless, not 'eight.'
findings.where: Sec VII (line 85): 'the body-witness and the both-ways-freed sit on the side that touches the eight liberations; the wisdom-freed and the rest sit on the side that does not (MN 70)'
findings.recommendation: Correct to: the both-ways-freed (and, in the broader roster, the body-witness) are marked by touching the FORMLESS liberations (santa vimokkha ... aruppa) with the body, per MN 70/SN 12.70. If 'eight liberations' is retained for the kayasakkhi, note that its canonical definition uses the full eight (incl. form-based) — a different formula from MN 70's aruppa clause — rather than silently treating the two as one.

findings.issue: 'the canon defines nibbana only in the negative, the unmade and the unborn' is one-sided. While Ud 8.3's 'ajatam abhutam akatam asankhatam' is genuinely privative, the Asankhata Samyutta (SN 43) supplies a large POSITIVE epithet-set for the unconditioned/nibbana — verified in the corpus at SN 43.44 and neighbours: siva (auspicious), khema (security), suddhi (purity), parayana (the goal), plus tana/lena/dipa/sarana (shelter/island/refuge) across the vagga; Dhp 203/204 calls it paramam sukham. 'Only in the negative' overstates the via negativa at the expense of a substantial positive vocabulary the canon also uses.
findings.where: Sec VII (line 63): 'the canon defines nibbana only in the negative, the unmade and the unborn, and declines to call it a place'
findings.recommendation: Soften to 'the canon's most distinctive nibbana-formula is privative (ajata/abhuta/akata/asankhata, Ud 8.3)' and acknowledge the positive epithet-cluster of the Asankhata Samyutta (SN 43) so the claim is 'prominently negative,' not 'only negative.' The 'declines to call it a place' point can stand on its own.

findings.issue: The claim that AN 9.12 sorts the nine persons 'by exactly this measure (*samadhismim mattaso kari*) ... mapping the gradient onto the grades of non-returner' is imprecise. In AN 9.12 (verbatim) the higher non-returner grades are 'samadhismim PARIPURAKARI' (concentration-FULFILLER) and vary only 'pannaya mattaso kari' (measure-doer in WISDOM); 'samadhismim mattaso kari' attaches to the LOWER grades (the once-returner/stream-enterer types in the nine). So the non-returners proper are concentration-fulfillers, not concentration-measure-doers, and the distinguishing axis among the nine is two-variable (samadhi x panna), not the single samadhi-measure the sentence implies.
findings.where: Sec VII (line 71): '*samadhismim mattaso kari* recurs where the canon sorts the nine noble persons who die "with a remainder" by exactly this measure (AN 9.12 ... which maps the gradient onto the grades of non-returner)'
findings.recommendation: Qualify: in AN 9.12 the non-returner grades are samadhi-fulfillers varying in wisdom; the samadhismim-mattaso-kari grade sits at the lower fruits. State that AN 9.12 deploys the same mattaso-kari idiom across a two-variable grid, rather than mapping the samadhi-measure specifically onto the non-returner grades.

findings.issue: The abstract glosses the kasina's canonical 'appamana' as 'a refusal of measure rather than a claim of the infinite.' In context the canonical formula is 'uddham adho tiriyam advayam appamanam' (MN 77, DN 33/34) — 'above, below, across, undivided, measureless' — describing all-directional pervasion without limit, which reads more naturally as boundless/unlimited than as a deliberate 'refusal of measure.' The interpretive distinction is the author's, imposed on a term the canon does not gloss that way; it risks over-reading to fit the 'open-becomes-bounded' thesis.
findings.where: Abstract (line 11): 'the kasina that the canon offers as a measureless perception (*appamana*, a refusal of measure rather than a claim of the infinite)'
findings.recommendation: Either drop the parenthetical interpretive contrast or flag it explicitly as an interpretation. The plain rendering ('measureless / boundless pervasion in all directions') already supports the open-vs-bounded point without the contestable 'refusal of measure' reading.

findings.issue: The cosmology and goal-of-the-path claims in the readable version (kasina-as-disc, cakkavala, kappavinasa, yojana/Meru, and especially 'nibbana defined only in the negative') extend well beyond what the rigor-track FINDINGS-v2.md actually coded — v2 is confined to the carita apparatus and its provenance signature, with no vimokkha/nibbana/cosmology coding. These expanded doctrinal claims therefore carry the readable register's authority without the dataset-level verification the carita core has, and two of them (eight liberations, nibbana-only-negative) are where the doctrinal errors above sit.
findings.where: Sec VII 'Where the hardening happens' (line 63) cosmology paragraph + abstract (line 11); contrast FINDINGS-v2.md, which codes only the carita signature
findings.recommendation: Bring the cosmology/nibbana/vimokkha claims under the same enumerate-and-sense-audit discipline as the carita core, or mark them in-text as a less-rigorously-coded extension. At minimum, fix the two doctrinal errors before these claims travel under the study's name.

findings.issue: The phrase 'the canon does imply something like a dry and a wet temperament, but only situationally' risks importing the very temperament framing the study elsewhere shows is late. AN 4.170 frames the four routes (samatha-first, vipassana-first, yuganaddha, dhamma-uddhacca) as situations to correct, not as person-kinds; calling them 'dry and wet temperaments' even with the 'situational' hedge invites the reading the study is arguing against.
findings.where: Sec VII 'Calm, insight...' (line 69): 'So the canon does imply something like a dry and a wet temperament, but only situationally'
findings.recommendation: Reword to keep the situational/process framing — e.g. 'a momentary lean toward calm or toward insight that the practice corrects' — and avoid 'temperament,' which the study has reserved for the late fixed-type sense.

reviews.lens: Methodology & quantitative critic — are the counts trustworthy, the census method sound, and the numbers stated honestly?
reviews.overall: The study's central empirical spine is sound and, where I could re-run it against the live corpus, honest: the headline temperament-compound = 0 in the four Nikāyas and Abhidhamma is real (rāgacarita et al. return 0 canon rows after the trigram recheck), cakkavāḷ confirms "almost absent from canon / over five hundred in commentary" (canon 2, commentary 559), and puggalavemattatā = 14 canon matches the supporting ledger. The method is genuinely careful in the ways that matter most for this kind of work: it disambiguates the headword from its homographs (sucarita/duccarita/caritvā), sense-audits abstract terms before trusting their counts (anusaya, cetopariya, paññāvimutta all correctly demoted), names the diacritic recall floor explicitly, and flags counter-examples rather than burying them. That places it well above a naive substring census. The weaknesses are quantitative-rhetoric weaknesses, not fabrication: (1) the corpus's single most-emphasized number, the sabhāva drift, is stated three different ways inside one document (and a fourth in the working notes) with no reconciliation — the live DB returns 2,086 for the commentary layer against the paper's "two thousand," "roughly 1,900," and a where-clause "1,906 / 553"; (2) the aggregate framing claims ("two dozen or so transitions," "most localize to a single jump," "a clear minority run against the trend") are never backed by an enumerated, auditable list with a stated denominator, so the most quotable structural claim of the paper is unfalsifiable as written; (3) several headline magnitudes are soft stem-counts (row-counts of passages containing a string, not occurrence counts, and edition-relative to paragraph segmentation) yet are narrated as if they were occurrence tallies; (4) the cosmology leg is genuinely thinner-evidenced than the person-guidance leg and leans on spot terms (kappavināsa, yojana, Meru) that are asserted more than counted in the prose. None of these overturn the thesis, but the paper currently claims more numerical precision and reproducibility than its own apparatus delivers.

findings.issue: The sabhāva commentary count — the paper's flagship 'corpus-wide drift' datum — is stated with three mutually inconsistent magnitudes inside this one document, and a fourth in the working notes, with no reconciliation. §VII says 'two thousand in the commentaries' and 'over four thousand in the sub-commentaries'; G1 prose says 'roughly 1,900 in the aṭṭhakathā and 3,950 in the ṭīkā'; the G1 where-clause says 'attha 1,906 / vism 553' (which sums to ~2,459 for the commentary layer, not 'two thousand'); DISCOVERY-PASS.md records 'attha 1573 / vism 513 / tika 4018'. A live re-query of the sabhāv stem across attha+vism returns 2,086. The numbers are all the same order of magnitude, but a reader cannot tell which is the real count, what stem truncation produced each, or why they differ, for the single number the study leans on hardest.
findings.where: §VII: 'from a handful of canonical occurrences to two thousand in the commentaries and over four thousand in the sub-commentaries'; G1: 'roughly 1,900 in the aṭṭhakathā and 3,950 in the ṭīkā ... sabhāva layer split, canon ≈ 0 / attha 1,906 / vism 553 / tika 3,951'
findings.recommendation: Pick one stem definition (e.g. sabhāv with the named false-positive compounds excluded), state the exact role-grouped row counts once in a single canonical table, and have §VII and G1 cite that table verbatim. If §VII's 'two thousand' is meant to be attha+vism combined, say so and make it match the where-clause (≈2,086–2,459). Reconcile against the live DB number before reprinting.

findings.issue: The paper's central structural claim is quantitative in form ('two dozen or so transitions counted this way, most localize to a single jump. A clear minority run against the trend') but no enumerated list of those transitions, no denominator, and no support/stray tally is given anywhere in the readable paper. 'Two dozen or so', 'most', and 'a clear minority' are presented as if they were counts but are not auditable: the reader cannot reconstruct which ~24 transitions were counted, how many localized vs. ran against the trend, or what 'most' and 'a clear minority' resolve to numerically. This is the claim the whole 'gradual, many-staged, tendency-not-law' conclusion rests on, and it is the least falsifiable sentence in the study.
findings.where: §VII 'Where the hardening happens': 'Of the two dozen or so transitions counted this way, most localize to a single jump. A clear minority run against the trend, and they are not few.'
findings.recommendation: Add an explicit enumerated transition ledger (term → layer-by-layer counts → classified as single-jump / multi-jump / counter-trend) either inline or as an appendix table, and state the actual split (e.g. 'N of M transitions localize to one jump; K run counter'). Replace 'two dozen or so' with the real N. Without the list, soften the prose to clearly signal it is an impression, not a count.

findings.issue:
Every 'count' in this corpus is a count of passages (rows) whose text contains a string, not a count of occurrences, and the rows are paragraph-segmented per the CST ingest. The prose repeatedly narrates these as occurrence tallies ('over five hundred occurrences', 'more than seven hundred times', 'over two hundred times', 'about ten to one', 'two hundred occurrences there against six'). A term appearing five times in one long paragraph counts once; the same term spread across five short paragraphs counts five. Because the commentary/sub-commentary were subdivided to per-

granularity (≈300–500 chars) while much canon remains coarser, the row-count ratios systematically inflate the late-layer side of every drift relative to a true occurrence count. The paper acknowledges edition-relativity in one line but then states the ratios as if they were occurrence facts.
findings.where: Abstract/§VII passim, e.g. 'becomes a commentarial fixture at over five hundred occurrences'; 'occurs four times in the four Nikāyas and then more than seven hundred times in the aṭṭhakathā'; 'jhānaṅga outnumbers its scattered canonical uses about ten to one'
findings.recommendation: State once, prominently, that all counts are passage/row counts not occurrence counts, and that paragraph segmentation differs by layer (commentary is finer-grained), which inflates late-layer row counts relative to occurrences. Either re-label every magnitude as 'rows' or compute true occurrence counts for the half-dozen headline terms. At minimum stop using the word 'occurrences' for what are row counts.

findings.issue: The paper asserts the counts are trivially reproducible ('Every count below is reproducible from the live database by a count grouped by textual role'; 'reconfirmed by direct SQL grouped by textual role'). In practice the documented query path does not reliably run: the live trigram index is on lower(COALESCE(original,'')), not on unaccent(original), so the recipe's unaccent(original) ILIKE queries cannot use any index and full-scan ~194K rows; a single diacritic-stem canon count (rāgacarita) took 55 s, and every multi-term batch I attempted hit the 120 s statement timeout. The numbers are recoverable with care, but the 'just run a count' framing oversells how clean and reproducible the pipeline is, and an independent checker following the stated recipe will mostly get timeouts.
findings.where: Method-in-brief: 'Every count below is reproducible from the live database by a count grouped by textual role, not by a search impression'; closing note: 'reconfirmed by direct SQL grouped by textual role'
findings.recommendation: Either add a functional index on unaccent(original) (or document that diacritic-inclusive lower(COALESCE(original,'')) LIKE patterns must be used to hit the trigram index), or ship the actual SQL + a frozen role-grouped count table as an appendix so reproduction does not depend on re-deriving working queries. Soften 'reproducible by a count' to reflect the real effort.

findings.issue: The cosmology leg is presented as 'as clean' as the person-guidance leg but is materially thinner. cakkavāḷa is the only cosmology term I could confirm has a real layer-split count. The others are asserted, not counted, in the prose: kappavināsa 'absent from the entire canon and is built in the commentaries' (a 0-canon claim with no count shown and which I could not verify — the query timed out), and 'counted in yojanas and Mount Meru and dated in world-ages' and the nibbāna 'declines to call it a place' claim are qualitative readings with no census behind them at all. A 0-canon claim is exactly the kind that needs a verified, correctly-truncated query (the handoff itself warns a 0 on an inflection-final stem is unsafe), yet kappavināsa's zero is stated bare.
findings.where: §VII: 'The description of the world follows the same line, and the counts are as clean ... The cycle by which a world is destroyed and reformed (kappavināsa) is absent from the entire canon and is built in the commentaries. What the early texts left measureless or hyperbolic is later counted in yojanas and Mount Meru and dated in world-ages.'
findings.recommendation: Provide verified role-grouped counts for kappavināsa (on a correctly-truncated root, not an inflection-final stem), yojana, and a Meru term, the same way carita and cakkavāḷa are counted; or downgrade 'the counts are as clean' to reflect that the cosmology leg rests on one counted term plus qualitative readings. The nibbāna/yojana/Meru claims should be marked as interpretive, not census, evidence.

findings.issue: The study's own working notes (HANDOFF-SAMADHI-EXHAUSTIVE) record that the count matrix used exact all_terms stems and therefore UNDERCOUNTS inflection-final stems, and that 'a 0 on an inflection-FINAL key is NOT safe (siblings can hide below it).' The readable paper inherits several bare 0-canon claims (upacāra-samādhi, khaṇika-samādhi, appanā-samādhi, samathayānika/vipassanāyānika/sukkhavipassaka, pādaka-jjhāna, the vehicle-split, kappavināsa) without indicating which were re-verified on a correctly-truncated root. The headline carita compound 0 I confirmed by re-query, but the reader is given no way to know which other zeros were truncation-checked.
findings.where: §VII '(upacāra, appanā) are named there for the first time, each zero before'; subsection 'How much concentration the lower path requires': 'access concentration (upacāra-samādhi), momentary concentration (khaṇika-samādhi), and absorption concentration as a named tier (appanā-samādhi) are each zero canonical rows'
findings.recommendation: Mark which 0-canon claims were re-verified on a truncated invariant root (per the study's own robustness rule) versus taken from the exact-stem matrix. A single appendix note 'all printed zeros re-checked on truncated roots on <date>' would discharge this.

findings.issue: Beyond 'two dozen or so', the paper repeatedly leans on hedged quantity words doing load-bearing structural work: 'A minority run the other way' (Abstract), 'A clear minority run against the trend, and they are not few' (§VII), 'most localize to a single jump'. These are the support/counter-case ratio that justifies the 'tendency not law' verdict, but the ratio is never pinned. 'Not few' and 'a clear minority' are in tension (is it few or not?) and cannot both be checked.
findings.where: Abstract: 'Most of these transitions localize to a single jump. A minority run the other way'; §VII: 'A clear minority run against the trend, and they are not few.'
findings.recommendation: Tie these to the enumerated transition ledger recommended above and give the actual support:counter ratio. If the ledger shows, say, ~17 single-jump vs ~8 counter/multi-jump, state those numbers; the rhetorical force of 'tendency with named exceptions' is stronger with the count than with the hedge.

findings.issue: Several precise within-Visuddhimagga fractions are stated that I could not re-verify and that invite scrutiny: 'of the fifty-three structurally-canonical rows that hold the disambiguated temperament compound, thirty are the Visuddhimagga', and 'the technical sense of kammaṭṭhāna ... is effectively confined there too (134 of 148 rows)'. These ratios depend on a 'structurally-canonical rows' construct and a 'disambiguated compound' filter that are not defined in the paper, and the kammaṭṭhāna count conflates the technical sense with the string. Without the filter definition the fractions are unfalsifiable.
findings.where: §V: 'of the fifty-three structurally-canonical rows that hold the disambiguated temperament compound, thirty are the Visuddhimagga, and the technical sense of kammaṭṭhāna ... is effectively confined there too (134 of 148 rows)'
findings.recommendation: Define 'structurally-canonical rows' and 'disambiguated temperament compound' where first used, and state how the 'technical sense' of kammaṭṭhāna was separated from non-technical uses of the string before the 134/148 ratio. Better, point these to the appendix census so they resolve.

findings.issue: The closing note grounds the study in 'the original carita study, 263 coded instances' at public/research/individual-guidance.json, but the narrative's drift counts (sabhāva thousands, cakkavāḷa hundreds, ajjhāsaya 701, etc.) are not part of that 263-instance coded dataset — they come from the later discovery pass and the live SQL, which are not machine-checkable in the same way (no per-row coding, no inter-annotator agreement on the sense-audit). The paper presents the carefully-coded census and the looser discovery-pass counts in one continuous quantitative voice, which lends the latter the former's rigor.
findings.where: Closing note: 'The full machine-checkable temperament census (the original carita study, 263 coded instances) ... The wide-recall discovery pass ... its verified rival-vocabulary ledger, the adversarial sense-audit, and the corrected-count register, are recorded in research/individual-guidance/DISCOVERY-PASS.md'
findings.recommendation: Distinguish in-text which numbers come from the 263-instance coded census (high rigor) versus the discovery-pass SQL counts (sense-audited but not per-row coded / no IAA), so the reader calibrates confidence accordingly. A one-line provenance tag per major count, or a 'census' vs 'survey count' typographic distinction, would suffice.

findings.category: gradient flagged 'partly-definitional' elsewhere but not here
findings.issue: The sibling Uttarakuru study's notes flag that coding chronological stratum partly determines the very gradient being measured ('gradient flagged partly-definitional'). The same circularity risk applies here: 'late' strata (commentary, sub-commentary) are defined by being the systematizing scholastic layers, so finding that systematization concentrates there is partly built into the layer assignment. The paper gestures at this in §VIII's closing self-caution but does not name the specific risk that the function-vs-essence axis and the stratum axis are not fully independent.
findings.where: §VIII closing: 'To trace a tradition's hardening into fixed systems is itself an act of systematization ... there is no neutral place to stand.'
findings.recommendation: Add one sentence acknowledging that stratum and 'fixedness' are not wholly independent constructs (the late layers are scholastic by definition), so the gradient is partly definitional — exactly as the sibling Uttarakuru study flags for its own place-literalness gradient.

reviews.lens: Logic & argumentation (non-sequiturs, overclaims, hidden assumptions, circularity, causal hedging)
reviews.overall: The study is, on the whole, argumentatively careful and unusually self-aware: it pre-empts its biggest threats (the function/essence axis is "offered as the most defensible description... not as a proof"; counter-cases are counted not waved away; drivers are explicitly left open; §VIII and §99 are genuine epistemic guardrails). The core empirical claim — the temperament-sense carita compound is absent from the four Nikayas and the seven Abhidhamma books and first fixes in the commentary — is a hard count and the conclusion that follows from it (the matching machinery is later than its "canonical shelving" implies) is sound. The strongest part is the concentration section, which is admirably hedged. The weaknesses are not in the headline but in the connective tissue: (1) the master "function-to-essence" frame is in places imposed rather than demonstrated — several phenomena (cosmology, jhana-factor analysis, gotrabhu) are assimilated to one axis by assertion, and the axis is partly definitional, which the paper concedes only glancingly; (2) the quantitative backbone of "most localize to a single jump, a minority run the other way" rests on an un-stated denominator and a never-specified counting rule, so the central distributional claim is not actually checkable from the text; (3) the move from "absent in the canon, present in commentary" to "the canon leaves X open / the canon asks less" sometimes slides from a lexical fact to a doctrinal one without bridging argument; and (4) some numbers in the published prose drift from the ledger they cite. None of these sink the study, but several should be tightened before it is pressed as hard as the abstract presses it.

findings.issue: The central distributional claim of the cross-layer section rests on a denominator and a counting rule that are never stated, so the claim is not checkable from the text. The paper says transitions are 'two dozen or so' and that 'most localize to a single jump' while 'a minority run the other way... and they are not few' / 'a clear minority run against the trend.' But there is no enumerated list of the ~24 transitions, no statement of how a 'transition' is individuated (why is jhana-factor-analysis one transition and not three?), and no count of how many 'localize to a single jump' vs how many are staged vs how many regress. 'Most,' 'a minority,' 'not few,' and 'a clear minority' are doing load-bearing work with no figures behind them. The reader cannot verify the headline 'staged not single, tendency not law' because the population it quantifies over is invisible.
findings.where: Abstract: 'Most of these transitions localize to a single jump. A minority run the other way' and §VII 'Where the hardening happens': 'Of the two dozen or so transitions counted this way, most localize to a single jump. A clear minority run against the trend, and they are not few.'
findings.recommendation: Either enumerate the ~24 transitions in a table (term, the jump where it hardens, single/staged/regressive) so 'most' and 'a minority' become countable, or soften the language to explicitly qualitative ('several,' 'a recurring handful') and drop the false precision of 'two dozen or so... most... a clear minority.' At minimum state the individuation rule for a 'transition.'

findings.issue: The 'function-to-essence' master axis is in several places asserted onto data rather than derived from it, and the paper concedes the axis is 'partly definitional' only obliquely. The cosmology material is the clearest case: a measureless kasina perception becoming a manufactured disc, an absent cakkavala becoming a fixture, yojana-counting, world-cycles — these are real lateness facts, but folding them under 'a present FUNCTION is set into a fixed ESSENCE' is a category stretch. A cosmological measure is not obviously a 'function' of anything; calling appamana->disc an instance of 'function->essence' uses 'essence' loosely to mean 'concrete/bounded,' which is a different axis (open->closed, measureless->measured) that the paper itself names a sentence later ('An open and measureless formulation is closed into a concrete and bounded one'). Two distinct axes (function/essence AND open/bounded) are being treated as 'one move... worked in two registers,' but the identity between them is assumed, not argued. Likewise gotrabhu (a technical moment becoming an Abhidhamma fixture) and the five-factor jhana analysis are absorbed into the same axis by placement, not by showing they are 'functions' frozen into 'essences.'
findings.where: Abstract: 'What stays consistently late is one move, worked in two registers. A present function is set into a fixed essence. An open and measureless formulation is closed into a concrete and bounded one.' and §VII: 'The same move that sets a present function into a standing type closes an open cosmos into a measured one.'
findings.recommendation: State explicitly that two related-but-distinct axes are in play (function->essence for persons/dhammas; measureless/open->bounded/measured for cosmos and quantity), and argue (don't assert) the claimed unity — or present them as a family of convergent late tendencies rather than 'one move.' Acknowledge that the unification is an interpretive synthesis, raising it from a glancing concession to an explicit claim the reader can contest.

findings.issue: Across the cosmology and concentration material the argument repeatedly moves from a LEXICAL absence (term X is zero in the canon) to a DOCTRINAL claim (the canon 'leaves open' / 'does not hold' / 'declines' the concept) without the bridging argument that absence-of-term entails absence-of-concept. The paper elsewhere flags exactly this danger for the carita case ('the silence of carita is the absence of a term, not the absence of person-typing,' §III; 'lexical absence != conceptual absence' in the ledger), which makes the unguarded use elsewhere inconsistent. E.g. kappavinasa 'absent from the entire canon and is built in the commentaries' is offered as evidence the canon left the world-cycle measureless, but the canon does carry world-contraction/expansion (sanvatta/vivatta) imagery; cakkavala's near-absence as a word does not by itself show the canon lacked a bounded-world notion. The inference 'term absent => concept open/absent' is precisely the diacritic/under-recall trap G3 warns about, deployed without the sense-audit the carita section insists on.
findings.where: §VII: 'The cycle by which a world is destroyed and reformed (kappavinasa) is absent from the entire canon and is built in the commentaries.' and the kasina/cakkavala sentences in the same paragraph.
findings.recommendation: For each cosmology term, either show (as was done for carita) that the CONCEPT and not just the WORD is canonically thin — e.g. note whether sanvatta/vivatta or measured-realm imagery already exists in the canon — or explicitly down-grade these to 'lexical' findings and state that the conceptual claim is weaker than the person-individuation one (which was sense-audited). Apply the §III standard ('absence of a term, not absence of the thing') uniformly.

findings.category: Numbers in prose drift from the cited ledger
findings.issue: The published sabhava figures are internally inconsistent and diverge from the source ledger. The abstract/§VII give 'two thousand in the commentaries and over four thousand in the sub-commentaries'; G1 gives 'roughly 1,900 in the attha and 3,950 in the tika.' The DISCOVERY-PASS ledger (§3/§4) records attha 1573 / vism 513 / tika 4018. So 'roughly 1,900' appears to silently fold vism (513) into attha (1573)=~2,086 in one place but report 1,906 in the G1 'Where' line, and 'two thousand'/'3,950'/'over four thousand' are three different roundings of the same two numbers. For a study whose whole method is 'every count is reproducible' and 'role-grouped not stem-grouped,' these casual re-roundings undercut the precision claim. The veneyya count is also given two ways ('over two hundred' in §V abstract-area vs '212' in G4).
findings.where: G1: 'roughly 1,900 in the attha and 3,950 in the tika... sabhava layer split, canon ~ 0 / attha 1,906 / vism 553 / tika 3,951' versus §VII 'two thousand in the commentaries and over four thousand in the sub-commentaries' and the ledger's attha 1573 / vism 513 / tika 4018.
findings.recommendation: Pick one set of role-grouped counts, state once whether 'commentaries' includes Visuddhimagga, and use that consistently. If the G1 'Where' numbers (1,906/553/3,951) are the reconfirmed SQL values, reconcile them with the ledger's 1573/513/4018 and explain the difference (re-query? unaccent? false-positive pruning?). The discrepancy itself should be resolved, not just harmonized in prose.

findings.category: Overclaim in abstract relative to hedged body
findings.issue: The abstract states the parenthetical gloss 'appamana, a refusal of measure rather than a claim of the infinite' as settled fact, and asserts the kasina 'that the canon offers as a measureless perception... becomes a manufactured disc.' The body (and the samadhi handoff) are more careful: the canon's kasina is a perception extended 'above, below, across, undivided, measureless' (MN 77), and the disc/nimitta apparatus is the commentary's technique. But 'a refusal of measure rather than a claim of the infinite' is an interpretive reading of appamana presented in the abstract with no hedge, and it is not obviously the only reading (appamana routinely glosses as 'immeasurable/boundless,' i.e. closer to 'the infinite' than to 'a refusal'). The abstract thus states an interpretive gloss as if it were a lexical given.
findings.where: Abstract: 'the kasina that the canon offers as a measureless perception (appamana, a refusal of measure rather than a claim of the infinite) becomes a manufactured disc.'
findings.recommendation: Hedge the appamana gloss in the abstract ('best read as a refusal of measure rather than...') or move the interpretive claim to the body where it can be supported. Do not let the abstract assert a contestable reading of a key term as definitional.

findings.category: Causal hedging — mostly good, one slip
findings.issue: Driver-language is generally well hedged ('It may answer to... It may answer to... That lies beyond what the texts can settle, and it is left open'). But one causal-sounding claim about oral transmission is asserted with a 'plain reason' that is actually a contestable premise: 'It is possible that an even simpler teaching was already being systematized in the centuries of oral transmission... for the plain reason that a teaching must be formulaic and listed to survive memorization at all.' The 'must... at all' is a strong necessity claim about memorization presented as self-evident ('the plain reason'), when it is an empirical hypothesis about oral culture, not a plain fact. This is the one place the otherwise-careful hedging lapses into treating a driver as obvious.
findings.where: §VIII: 'It is possible that an even simpler teaching was already being systematized in the centuries of oral transmission before writing, for the plain reason that a teaching must be formulaic and listed to survive memorization at all.'
findings.recommendation: Downgrade 'the plain reason that a teaching must... at all' to 'on the plausible assumption that' or similar. The conditional ('If so...') that follows is fine; it is the asserted necessity premise feeding it that needs the same hedge applied to every other driver.

findings.category: Potential circularity in the master-axis framing
findings.issue: There is a mild circularity risk in how the axis is selected and then used to grade the evidence. §VII says 'the field above is a story about one axis more than about chronology,' and the function/essence axis is chosen because it is 'the axis the data cleaves on most sharply' (§VIII). But the same axis is then used to decide which phenomena count as confirming instances (temperament, vehicles, jhana-factors, cosmology all get read as function->essence) and which count as 'counter-cases' (puggalavemattata declining, Abhidhamma carrying a present-tense reading forward). When the categorizing axis also defines what is a confirmation vs a counter-case, the 'strong tendency with named exceptions' verdict is partly guaranteed by the framing. The paper's honesty about this ('once seen this way the scheme stops being the headline and becomes an example') actually surfaces the risk rather than dispelling it.
findings.where: §VII: 'the field above is a story about one axis more than about chronology... The six-temperament scheme is one instance of it, and once seen this way the scheme stops being the headline and becomes an example.' with §VIII 'The function-versus-essence reading is the axis the data cleaves on most sharply.'
findings.recommendation: Add one sentence acknowledging the selection effect: the axis was chosen post hoc as the sharpest cleavage, so the counter-case/confirmation tally is relative to that choice and a different axis might re-sort some items. This is implied by 'offered as the most defensible description... not as a proof' but should be made explicit at the point where confirmations and counter-cases are tallied, since that is where the circularity bites.

findings.category: Reflexive note — coherent but slightly self-immunizing
findings.issue: The reflexive closing (§99) is coherent and not self-undermining in the fatal sense (it does not refute its own findings), but it edges toward self-immunization: it lists exactly the three guardrails the paper already uses ('reported as a tendency and not a law... counter-cases are counted... drivers are left open') as the answer to the charge that the study itself systematizes. That makes the reflexive note function less as a genuine check and more as a restatement of the paper's virtues — it answers 'are you doing to the texts what the commentators did?' with 'no, because I did the things I did,' which is close to begging the question. The raft simile (MN 22) is apt but rhetorical, not an argument that the reduction-to-counts is warranted.
findings.where: §99: 'The guard against doing to the texts what the commentators are charged with doing is built into the findings rather than asserted over them. The pattern is reported as a tendency and not a law. The counter-cases are counted rather than waved away. The proposed drivers are left open.'
findings.recommendation: Strengthen the reflexive note by naming a concrete cost of the reduction (what the count-and-axis method demonstrably cannot see, e.g. a passage where the function/essence sorting does violence to the text), rather than only re-listing the paper's hedges. A reflexive note is stronger when it concedes a specific blind spot than when it certifies the method's own caution.

findings.issue: The 'argument from the ceiling' is explicitly and well hedged (the paper itself notes 'the weight... is carried less by this a-fortiori than by the gradient, the negative space, and the caused-fruit account'), so this is near-fine. But the a-fortiori as stated has a gap the hedge does not fully close: 'if even arahantship can be reached without the formless attainments that lie beyond the four jhanas, the lower two fruits can a fortiori dispense with them.' That the lower fruits can dispense with the FORMLESS attainments is nearly trivial and not the contested question; the contested question is whether they can dispense with the FOUR JHANAS, which this a-fortiori does not touch. So the a-fortiori proves a point almost no one disputes and is silent on the one in dispute — its inclusion risks reading as more support for the lower-fruit-floor claim than it actually provides.
findings.where: §85: 'So the secure point is bounded: if even arahantship can be reached without the formless attainments that lie beyond the four jhanas, the lower two fruits can a fortiori dispense with them.'
findings.recommendation: State plainly that this a-fortiori settles only the formless attainments (the uncontested part) and contributes little to the contested four-jhana floor, so the reader does not over-weight it. The paper half-does this; make it unambiguous that the a-fortiori is not evidence on the live question.

findings.issue: The abstract's and conclusion's closing move — 'For the practical matter this began with, the lower reaches of the path ask less than the later lore implies... So is the cosmos it sits within' — yokes the practical concentration conclusion to the cosmology conclusion with a 'So is' that implies parallel evidential strength. But the concentration claim is heavily argued (four structural facts, sense-audited) while the cosmology claim is, by the study's own evidence, mostly lexical (term-absence counts). Ending on 'So is the cosmos' lends the weaker cosmology strand the rhetorical authority of the stronger concentration strand.
findings.where: Abstract/Conclusion: 'the lower reaches of the path ask less than the later lore implies. The machinery that fits a person to a practice is later and firmer in its claims than its canonical shelving suggests. So is the cosmos it sits within.'
findings.recommendation: Either drop the 'So is the cosmos' coda from the conclusion or flag that the cosmology strand is the more lightly-evidenced of the two, so the parallelism does not over-sell it.

findings.issue: puggalavemattata is offered as the cleanest counter-case ('the explicit declaration that persons differ... is loudest in the early canon and fades in the later layers'), but the ledger records it at only 14 canon-mula rows and '2 para / 4 attha / 0 vism / 2 tika.' A term that goes from 14 to single digits is a thin reed to carry 'the divergence... seen from the side of the count,' and the smallness is not disclosed in the readable prose. A counter-case resting on ~14 rows deserves the same 'floors recovered by widening search' caveat the confirmations get.
findings.where: §VII: 'one canonical signature runs the other way entirely: the explicit declaration that persons differ (puggalavemattata) is loudest in the early canon and fades in the later layers.'
findings.recommendation: Disclose the raw smallness of the puggalavemattata counts (it is ~14 canon rows) in the readable text, as the rigor lane presumably does, so the counter-case is not read as a larger signal than it is.

reviews.overall: As one study it mostly coheres around a single, genuinely defensible axis — function hardening into essence — and the persons domain is densely evidenced across six strata with sense-auditing, counter-cases counted, and an unusually honest Limits section. The problem the broadening introduced is asymmetry of evidence and architecture. The persons axis gets seven sections and two deep-dive subsections; the cosmos axis gets roughly two paragraphs of bare counts with no dedicated section, no sense-audit narrative, and no sampled-row defense, even though the Method-in-brief explicitly promises that "every term is read in sampled rows before it is trusted" and that promise is only ever demonstrated for the persons vocabulary. The cosmology reads as appended to an existing persons study to license the retitle, not as a co-equal investigation. The result is a study whose thesis is broader than its evidentiary base: the abstract and conclusion assert a two-register law ("worked in two registers"), but the body only earns one register to the study's own standard. The title fits the axis but undersells/obscures the domains, and several abstract-level cosmology claims (nibbāna realms, kappavināsa, gotrabhū) appear nowhere in a developed form. None of this is fatal — the core finding survives — but the cosmos claims should be downgraded to "consistent with, on uninspected counts" or given a real section, and the abstract should stop claiming symmetry it does not deliver.

findings.issue: The cosmology axis — half the retitled study's stated subject — is evidenced almost entirely by bare term counts in two paragraphs (the abstract and the §VII 'Where the hardening happens' subsection). There is no dedicated section for it, and crucially no sense-audit. The Method-in-brief promises 'every term is read in sampled rows before it is trusted' and the persons axis visibly honors this (the carita over-recall of 224 conduct-words, the anusaya/cetopariya exclusions). For cakkavāḷa, appamāṇa, kappavināsa, yojana, sabhāva-as-cosmos, no equivalent sampled-row defense is shown. A raw count of cakkavāḷa at 500+ is exactly the 'abstract term's raw count is often a fixed doctrinal list' trap the study warns about elsewhere, yet the trap is never checked on the cosmos side.
findings.where: §VII 'Where the hardening happens': 'The bounded world-sphere (cakkavāḷa), almost absent from the canon, becomes a commentarial fixture at over five hundred occurrences. The cycle by which a world is destroyed and reformed (kappavināsa) is absent from the entire canon and is built in the commentaries.'
findings.recommendation: Either give the cosmos axis its own short section with the same sense-audit discipline applied to persons (sample cakkavāḷa/appamāṇa/yojana rows, distinguish literal-cosmology uses from idiom), or explicitly demote the cosmos claims in the abstract and conclusion to 'consistent on uninspected counts, not sense-audited to the same standard as the persons axis.' Do not let an unaudited count carry a co-headline claim.

findings.issue: The abstract asserts a symmetric two-register thesis as established fact ('What stays consistently late is one move, worked in two registers'), and the conclusion repeats it. But the body only develops one register (persons) to the study's evidentiary standard. The abstract thereby overstates what the body delivers: it presents the cosmos register as equally demonstrated when it is, in the body, a thinly-sourced extension. This is the classic 'abstract claims more than the results section earns' defect.
findings.where: Abstract: 'What stays consistently late is one move, worked in two registers. A present function is set into a fixed essence. An open and measureless formulation is closed into a concrete and bounded one.'
findings.recommendation: Hedge the abstract to match the body's actual weight, e.g. 'demonstrated in detail for the guidance of persons and shown to extend, on parallel term-counts, to the description of the world.' Reserve the flat two-register assertion for the conclusion only if the cosmos section is built out.

findings.category: Abstract claims with no body support
findings.issue: Several specific cosmology claims surface in the abstract or the cross-layer paragraph and are never developed, cited to a row, or revisited. 'dated by world-cycles', the nibbāna-as-place contrast, and the gotrabhū datum each appear once as an assertion. The nibbāna claim ('the canon defines nibbāna only in the negative... while the later cosmology fills the afterworld with tiered and measured realms') is a large doctrinal claim dropped in a single sentence with no citation and no counting, unlike every persons-side claim which carries a row figure or sutta id.
findings.where: §VII: 'Even the goal is not quite exempt: the canon defines nibbāna only in the negative, the unmade and the unborn, and declines to call it a place, while the later cosmology fills the afterworld with tiered and measured realms.'
findings.recommendation: Either attach the same row-count / citation apparatus the persons claims carry (which terms, which strata, sampled rows) or cut these to a clearly-flagged 'plausibly parallel, not investigated here' aside. As written they import the authority of the counted claims without the counting.

findings.issue: The study states the hardening 'happens at four separate jumps' but a few paragraphs later refers to 'two dozen or so transitions counted this way, most localize to a single jump.' These two framings sit uneasily: 'four jumps' (Abhidhamma, para-canonical, commentary, sub-commentary boundaries) describes where-in-the-ladder, while 'two dozen transitions... most localize to a single jump' describes individual term-trajectories. A reader cannot reconcile 'four jumps' with 'two dozen transitions, most at a single jump' without the author spelling out that these count different things. The abstract compounds this with 'it recurs at more than one jump,' a third, vaguer figure.
findings.where: §VII: 'It happens at four separate jumps' vs. 'Of the two dozen or so transitions counted this way, most localize to a single jump.'
