findings.recommendation: Define the two senses explicitly: the ladder has four boundary-jumps; ~24 individual term-trajectories were tracked; most of those trajectories concentrate their movement at one of those four boundaries. State the distinction in one sentence before using both counts.

findings.issue: 'From Function to Essence' names the analytic axis cleanly but signals nothing about the persons+cosmos breadth that the retitle was meant to capture; the entire load of 'this is now two domains' rests on the subtitle ('in the guidance of persons and the description of the world'). Given that the cosmos domain is the weaker-evidenced half, a title that foregrounds the axis and buries the domains in the subtitle actually flatters the imbalance rather than exposing it. The title is defensible but does not, on its own, tell a reader this is a two-domain study.
findings.where: Title: 'From Function to Essence' / subtitle: 'How the early Buddhist teaching is systematized across the textual layers, in the guidance of persons and the description of the world.'
findings.recommendation: Acceptable to keep if the cosmos section is strengthened. If it is not strengthened, the title-plus-subtitle overpromises a symmetry the study does not have; consider a subtitle that marks the cosmos strand as the corroborating extension rather than a co-equal domain.

findings.issue: The two longest analytic units in the study — the §VII subsections 'Calm, insight, and how much concentration the path asks' and 'How much concentration the lower path requires' — are a deep dive into a single practice-scholarship controversy (jhana requirement for stream-entry). This material is excellent but it is a study unto itself; it dwarfs the cosmos treatment and arguably dwarfs the temperament census that the study is nominally about. The center of gravity has migrated from 'how the canon matches a person to a practice' to 'how much concentration the lower fruits require,' which the abstract only gestures at in its final two sentences. The study coheres thematically (it is all function-to-essence) but its proportions no longer match its stated question.
findings.where: §VII subsections 'Calm, insight, and how much concentration the path asks' and 'How much concentration the lower path requires' (the document's two largest analytic blocks, lines 67-87).
findings.recommendation: Either elevate the concentration question into the abstract and framing as a co-equal finding (it is treated as one in the body), or tighten these two subsections so they do not structurally outweigh both the temperament census and the cosmos axis. Right now the abstract under-advertises the study's actual largest contribution.

findings.category: Cosmos axis — 'same logic' asserted, not shown
findings.issue: The abstract claims 'The description of the world hardens by the same logic' and §VII claims 'The same move that sets a present function into a standing type closes an open cosmos into a measured one.' But function-to-essence (a present verb-state frozen into a standing type) and measureless-to-measured (a refusal of measure closed into a yojana count) are not obviously the same move — they share a direction (open to closed) but the persons axis is about reification of a disposition into a type, whereas the cosmos axis is about quantification of an indefinite into a number. Calling them 'the same logic' is an equation the study asserts but never argues. It may be right, but it is doing real thesis-unifying work on an assertion.
findings.where: Abstract: 'The description of the world hardens by the same logic.' and §VII: 'The same move that sets a present function into a standing type closes an open cosmos into a measured one.'
findings.recommendation: Add one or two sentences arguing why reification-into-type and quantification-of-the-measureless are instances of one move (e.g. both substitute a fixed determinate for an open indeterminate), rather than asserting the identity. This is the hinge that makes it one study rather than two; it deserves an argued sentence.

findings.issue: Every persons-axis count in the body is either given a row figure with stratum split (e.g. ajjhāsaya '4 times... more than seven hundred', sabhāva 'canon ≈ 0 / attha 1,906 / vism 553 / tika 3,951') or a sutta id. The cosmos counts are given only as round approximations ('over five hundred occurrences', 'counted in yojanas and Mount Meru and dated in world-ages') with no stratum table and no anchor in the closing reproducibility note, which lists only the persons/sabhava data path and the carita json. A reader cannot reproduce the cosmos counts from what is provided.
findings.where: Closing note: 'The full machine-checkable temperament census... is at public/research/individual-guidance.json' — no equivalent ledger pointer for the cosmology counts (cakkavāḷa, kappavināsa, yojana, appamāṇa).
findings.recommendation: Add the cosmos term counts to the DISCOVERY-PASS ledger and reference them in the closing reproducibility note, or explicitly state the cosmos counts are illustrative and not part of the frozen, machine-checkable dataset.

findings.category: Findings of general importance — domain drift
findings.issue: The four 'Findings of general importance' (G1-G4) are all drawn from the persons/vocabulary side (sabhava drift, assortative association, the diacritic search trap, describe-early-name-late). None arises from the cosmology axis. For a study that retitled itself to foreground the cosmos, the absence of any cosmos-derived general finding is a quiet confirmation that the cosmos material was not investigated deeply enough to yield one. G1 (sabhava) is the closest, and it is framed as person-vocabulary drift, not cosmology.
findings.where: §'Findings of general importance': G1-G4, all sourced from the persons/term-search side; e.g. G3 'A reusable diacritic-and-substring trap in corpus search' and G4 'A describe-early, name-late signature in scholastic terminology.'
findings.recommendation: Either surface a genuine cosmos-axis general finding (e.g. the appamāṇa 'refusal of measure' mis-rendered as 'infinite' is itself a reusable translation-drift datum) or accept that the general findings reveal the study's true center is the persons/vocabulary work, and align the abstract's symmetry claim accordingly.

findings.issue: Three different carita-related figures appear (224 raw conduct-word rows in canon; 53 'structurally-canonical rows that hold the disambiguated temperament compound' of which 30 are Visuddhimagga; 263 coded instances in the appendix dataset). These are reconcilable (raw substring vs disambiguated-compound vs full coded census across all strata) but the study never states the relationship, so a careful reader hits an apparent contradiction between 'attested zero times in the four Nikāyas and the seven Abhidhamma books' and '53 structurally-canonical rows' without being told 'structurally-canonical' here includes the para-canonical Khuddaka and that the zero is specifically Nikaya+Abhidhamma.
findings.where: §V: 'of the fifty-three structurally-canonical rows that hold the disambiguated temperament compound, thirty are the Visuddhimagga' vs Abstract: 'attested zero times in the four Nikāyas and the seven Abhidhamma books.'
findings.recommendation: Add a half-sentence clarifying that 'structurally-canonical' bundles the para-canonical Khuddaka (where the compound first appears) and that the 'zero' claim is scoped precisely to the four Nikāyas plus the seven Abhidhamma books, so the two figures are not in tension.

reviews.lens: Adversarial peer review — devil's advocate against the function-to-essence "hardening" thesis
reviews.overall: The study is genuinely strong on its hardest, most defensible point: the temperament-sense *carita* compound is a categorical zero in the four Nikāyas and the seven Abhidhamma books, reconfirmed by SQL, and that single hard lexical absence is the load-bearing fact the whole apparatus argument can rest on. The discovery pass and the under-recall correction (the seven-puggala roster hiding under diacritics) are real methodological merits. But the paper's larger thesis — that this is one instance of a unified "function-to-essence hardening" recurring across strata in two domains — is considerably softer than its prose admits, and a determined skeptic can mount three serious lines of attack. (1) The numbers are not internally consistent: the same quantity (sabhāva commentary occurrences, the carita compound row count) carries materially different values across the three supporting documents and the paper, which means the "as clean as the counts" rhetoric is not earned. (2) The central interpretive claim — that later elaboration is *essentialization* rather than *mere elaboration/explicitation* — is asserted by word-choice ("freezes", "hardens", "fixed essence") and never tested against the obvious null that a tradition naturally coins technical nouns for practices it inherited (which is exactly what G4 describes, neutrally). The same data supports a deflationary "the tradition got more explicit and more systematic over time" reading that carries none of the non-self/own-nature irony the conclusion leans on. (3) The pattern is partly a selection effect: the set of ~27 "transitions" was hand-chosen by agents looking for the pattern, the strays are counted but then verbally demoted ("a minority", "named exceptions"), and the supporting census actually scores only ~63% clean support — a number the readable abstract never gives the reader. The strays (puggalavemattatā peaking early, Abhidhamma preservation, the Vism self-disavowal) are not noise around a law; at one-third of cases they are nearly large enough to be a rival description. The study is honest enough to count its counter-cases, which is to its credit, but it then writes them down in the prose. None of this refutes the carita core; it does undercut the grander two-domain "hardening" framing the title and abstract sell.

findings.issue: The sabhāva counts that anchor finding G1 (the corpus-wide essentialization claim) differ materially across the paper's own supporting documents. The readable paper's G1 gives 'attha 1,906 / vism 553 / tika 3,951'; the abstract-level prose rounds to '1,900 ... 3,950'; DISCOVERY-PASS.md gives 'attha 1573 / vism 513 / tika 4018'; HARDENING-CENSUS.md gives 'comm 2004 / tika 4406'. These are the same lexeme over the same corpus and the spread is ~25-30%. For a study whose rhetorical force is 'every count comes from a query grouped on textual stratum' and 'the counts are as clean', an unreconciled three-way disagreement on its flagship number is a credibility problem.
findings.where: G1: 'roughly 1,900 in the aṭṭhakathā and 3,950 in the ṭīkā, against a canonical floor near zero' vs DISCOVERY-PASS 'attha 1573 / vism 513 / tika 4018' vs HARDENING-CENSUS 'comm 2004 / tika 4406'
findings.recommendation: Re-run the sabhāva count once, freeze the exact SQL and the stem/dedup rule used, and make every document cite that single frozen number. State explicitly which compounds were excluded (purisabhāva, ekaṃsa-bhāvita) and how, since the magnitude of G1 depends entirely on that exclusion being right.

findings.issue: The paper's core claim is that later layers 'freeze a function into an essence' — but it never operationalizes the difference between essentialization and ordinary elaboration/explicitation, and never states what evidence would distinguish them. A skeptic can read every cited transition as a tradition doing the unremarkable work of coining technical nouns and closed lists for practices it inherited (which is precisely what finding G4, 'describe-early, name-late', states in neutral language). On that reading there is no 'hardening toward fixed essence' at all, just normal scholastic systematization. The loaded verbs ('freezes', 'hardens', 'fixed essence', 'physiology') do the interpretive work that the data does not compel. The non-self irony of the conclusion ('a teaching of non-self ... comes to rest on the language of own-nature') only lands if the essentialization reading is privileged, and that reading is asserted, not demonstrated.
findings.where: Conclusion: 'A present function is set into a fixed essence.' and §VII: 'The deepest pattern is the move from the first to the second, the freezing of a function into an essence.'
findings.recommendation: Either pre-commit to a discriminating test (e.g. show that the late terms carry ontological commitment the early practice lacked, not just a noun where there was a verb) or explicitly down-grade the claim to the deflationary version that G4 already supports: the tradition nominalized and systematized. The sabhāva/own-nature evidence is the only leg that genuinely speaks to ontology; the carita/jhānaṅga/upacāra evidence speaks to systematization, which is a weaker and different claim. Do not let the one ontology term (sabhāva) color all the systematization terms.

findings.issue: The supporting HARDENING-CENSUS.md scores the trend at '~17 support / ~8 stray / ~2 mixed ... ≈63% clean support', and concludes H1 is 'substantially supported but NOT universal' and 'H0 is partly true'. The readable paper systematically softens this. The abstract says only 'A minority run the other way'; §VII says 'most localize to a single jump. A clear minority run against the trend, and they are not few.' One-third of cases (8 of ~27) running against or sideways to a thesis is not naturally described as 'a minority' supporting the thesis — it is close to the threshold where a reviewer would ask whether the rival description ('an already-systematic canon that the commentary elaborates unevenly') fits as well. The reader of the readable paper is never given the 63% figure or the 'H0 is partly true' verdict.
findings.where: Abstract: 'A minority run the other way' and §VII: 'most localize to a single jump. A clear minority run against the trend, and they are not few.' — vs HARDENING-CENSUS: '≈ 63% clean support' and 'H0 is partly true'.
findings.recommendation: Report the actual fraction (e.g. 'roughly two-thirds of the counted transitions localize to a single hardening jump; the remaining third are early system, preservation, or regression') in the readable paper, not just in the internal census. Let the reader weigh whether two-thirds is a 'strong tendency' or a 'majority pattern with a large dissenting minority'.

findings.issue: The thesis is vulnerable to exactly the critique the discovery pass was built to answer, only one level up. The discovery pass fixed the 'searched one word' problem for the person-guidance domain, but the cross-domain 'hardening' claim rests on a hand-assembled set of ~27 transitions chosen by agents already looking for function→essence. The census admits 'Invented probe numbers ... are unreproducible' and that several candidate families failed the sense-audit and were pruned. A set curated by pattern-seekers, with failed candidates removed, will over-represent the pattern. There is no pre-registered, neutral enumeration of 'all technical terms that change frequency across strata' from which these 27 were drawn, so the denominator is unknown and the 63% support rate is computed over a numerator-friendly sample.
findings.where: §VII: 'Of the two dozen or so transitions counted this way, most localize to a single jump.' and method note: 'the wider reading searched the rival vocabularies ... every such family was sense-audited'; DISCOVERY-PASS §5: 'Invented probe numbers ... are unreproducible'
findings.recommendation: Acknowledge the selection risk explicitly in the Limits section: the transition set is a curated sample, not a census of the corpus's vocabulary, so the support fraction describes the chosen terms, not the corpus. Ideally name a neutral selection rule (e.g. all terms above a frequency threshold that are zero in early canon) and report how the chosen 27 fared against it.

findings.issue: Two different row counts for 'the temperament compound' sit in the paper and its sources without reconciliation, and a careful reader will collide with them. §V says 'of the fifty-three structurally-canonical rows that hold the disambiguated temperament compound, thirty are the Visuddhimagga' (and FINDINGS-v2 agrees: 53 mula rows, all Vism or para-canonical). But HARDENING-CENSUS gives the rāgacarita stem a six-stratum distribution of 0/12/0/9/58/43, i.e. ~58 commentary rows alone and ~122 total. The paper never explains that '53' is the structural-mula subset while '58' is the commentary structural-layer population — so the same phrase ('the temperament compound') appears to be both 53-total and 58-in-commentary-alone. This looks like a contradiction even though it is reconcilable as two different populations (structural layer vs stratum).
findings.where: §V: 'of the fifty-three structurally-canonical rows that hold the disambiguated temperament compound, thirty are the Visuddhimagga' vs HARDENING-CENSUS rāgacarita row '0 | 12 | 0 | 9 | 58 | 43'
findings.recommendation: State once, in plain terms, that 'structurally-mula rows' and 'commentary-stratum rows' are different cuts of the same data and give both numbers side by side, or pick one population for the headline. The phrase 'structurally-canonical rows' is itself confusing — a Visuddhimagga row is not canonical, so explain that 'structural mula' is a database role, not a canonicity claim.

findings.issue: Every headline rests on 'zero in the canon' claims, yet the method note and the samādhi handoff both concede the counts are diacritic/stem-fragile floors. HANDOFF-SAMADHI-EXHAUSTIVE warns 'a 0 on an inflection-FINAL key is NOT safe (siblings can hide below it)'. HARDENING-CENSUS itself caught one: the saragaṃ cittaṃ early=0 was 'an artifact (the canonical refrain inserts vā)'. The paper presents the zeros as hard categorical facts ('attested zero times', 'flatly zero', 'each zero before') with far more confidence than a self-described floor-count over a morphologically rich corpus warrants. The carita-compound zero is defensible because it was specifically hunted with prefixed defilement stems; many of the other zeros (upacāra/appanā/khaṇika as a tier, gotrabhū, jhānaṅga) are asserted with the same flat confidence but it is not shown they were each truncation-verified.
findings.where: Abstract: 'attested zero times in the four Nikāyas and the seven Abhidhamma books' and §VII: 'Access and absorption concentration (upacāra, appanā) are named there for the first time, each zero before' — vs HARDENING-CENSUS recall caveat: 'saragaṃ cittaṁ early=0 is an artifact'
findings.recommendation: Distinguish in the prose between the one zero that is genuinely robust (the temperament compound, hunted with all six defilement-prefixed stems) and the other zeros, which are floors. A single sentence — 'these zeros are recovered by truncated-stem search and are floors, not proofs of total absence' — already exists in the method note but should gate the confident 'flatly zero' phrasings in the body.

findings.issue: The whole thesis depends on the six-stratum ordering being a chronology, but the paper concedes the ordering is 'relative order, not calendar date' and the strata are assigned by genre/redaction-layer consensus. The argument that the apparatus is 'late' is therefore partly the argument that commentaries are later than suttas — true, but the move from 'appears in a later-redacted layer' to 'hardening over time' quietly assumes the layers were not all available to the early community in oral form. The paper's own §VIII raises this ('an even simpler teaching was already being systematized in the centuries of oral transmission') and then sets it aside, but that concession is more corrosive to the thesis than its placement suggests: if systematization predates the written record, then 'the canon individuates by function, the commentary by essence' may be a genre/register difference (what gets written in a sutta vs a manual) rather than a temporal hardening.
findings.where: §VIII: 'The dating is relative order, not calendar date' and 'It is possible that an even simpler teaching was already being systematized in the centuries of oral transmission before writing'
findings.recommendation: Foreground the genre-vs-chronology alternative as a live rival explanation, not just a limit. A sutta and a meditation manual differ in register by design; some of the 'function early / essence late' split may be that suttas are not the genre where closed lists live, regardless of date. The carita zero survives this (a manual term simply absent from the Abhidhamma's sorting layer is telling), but the broader two-domain trend is more exposed to it.

findings.issue: The abstract claims the move is 'worked in two registers' (person-guidance and world-description), presenting the cosmology domain as independent confirmation. But the world-description evidence is thinner and partly definitional. The strongest cosmology items (cakkavāḷa, kappavināsa, yojana-counting) are about cosmological vocabulary that has no early-canonical occasion to appear, not about a function being essentialized — a bounded world-sphere is not a 'function' that 'hardened', it is simply new content. Treating the cosmology drift as the same move as the carita freeze risks equivocation: 'measureless becomes measured' (appamāṇa kasiṇa → disc) is one logic; 'absent becomes present' (cakkavāḷa, kappavināsa built in the commentaries) is just accretion of new doctrine, which every growing tradition does and which carries no essentialization claim.
findings.where: Abstract: 'It is not a single break between canon and commentary ... worked in two registers' and §VII: 'The cycle by which a world is destroyed and reformed (kappavināsa) is absent from the entire canon and is built in the commentaries.'
findings.recommendation: Separate the two sub-claims in the cosmology domain: (a) measureless→measured (genuinely parallel to function→essence) and (b) absent→present (mere doctrinal accretion). Only (a) supports the unified thesis; (b) is a different and weaker phenomenon. Do not let new-content accretion be counted as 'hardening'.

findings.category: the puggalavemattatā stray is stronger than admitted
findings.issue: The single cleanest counter-signature — puggalavemattatā 'peaks early, fades late' (15/1/0/0/4/2 per HARDENING-CENSUS) — is the explicit canonical *naming* of difference among persons, and it runs exactly opposite to the thesis (the canon is more explicit about typed difference than the commentary). The paper mentions it but frames it as one of three 'exceptions' rather than confronting that it is the most on-the-nose test case: where the canon does coin an abstract noun for person-difference, that noun declines over time. This is a datum that the 'canon=function, commentary=essence' axis does not predict and arguably mildly contradicts.
findings.where: §VII: 'one canonical signature runs the other way entirely: the explicit declaration that persons differ (puggalavemattatā) is loudest in the early canon and fades in the later layers'
findings.recommendation: Give this its own short treatment rather than burying it in a list of three exceptions, and address head-on why the one early abstract noun for typed difference declines — does it weaken the nominalization-is-late story? At minimum acknowledge it as the case the axis least predicts.

findings.category: circular use of the Vism self-disavowal
findings.issue: The Visuddhimagga's 'na sārato paccetabbaṃ' disclaimer is deployed twice as a load-bearing rhetorical climax — the tradition disowning its own apparatus. But the disclaimer is narrowly about one diagnostic *method* (reading temperament from gait/eating/sleeping), not about the temperament typology itself, which the Vism fully endorses and builds on. The paper occasionally lets the disclaimer's force bleed onto the whole apparatus ('the matching machinery is later, and softer in its own claims'), when in fact the Vism is confident about the six types and the forty subjects and hedges only the outward read-off technique. Using a local methodological caveat as evidence that the whole grid is 'softer in its own claims' overreaches.
findings.where: §VIII: 'the tradition's own apex manual, having set the grid out in full, declines to certify its most detailed part' and 'The matching machinery is later, and softer in its own claims'
findings.recommendation: Tighten the scope: the Vism hedges the diagnostic read-off, not the typology. The honest version is 'the tradition certifies the types but not the outward method of assigning them', which is a narrower and more accurate claim than 'the machinery is softer in its own claims'.

findings.issue: The abstract opens with a sweeping universalized binary ('The Pāli canon's earliest layer teaches in one register. Its later literature teaches in another.') that the body then spends pages qualifying into a non-monotonic tendency with a one-third dissent rate. The opening overstates what the study concludes and primes the reader to read the qualified findings as confirmation of a cleaner claim than the data supports.
findings.where: Abstract: 'The Pāli canon's earliest layer teaches in one register. Its later literature teaches in another.'
findings.recommendation: Soften the opening to match the conclusion's 'gradual, many-staged, tendency-not-law' verdict, so the abstract does not promise a binary the body retracts.

reviews.lens: Citation and evidence critic — does each cited locus actually support the claim, is every number sourced, and does any "the canon says" rest on a single passage?
reviews.overall: From the citation-and-evidence lens the study is, on its spine, unusually sound. I resolved all 32 sampled cited sutta IDs against the live corpus (194,710 rows): every one exists, with a title and content matching its use (AN 9.3 = Meghiyasutta addressed to Meghiya; PS 3.5 = Cariyākathā carrying the eight cariyā verbatim; SN 14.14 opening with the dhātuso-saṃsandanti homophily formula; CND 19 = Mogharājamāṇavapucchāniddesa; AN 4.133 = the four learners; AN 7.14 = the seven persons). The single most load-bearing claim — the temperament compound is attested 0 times in the four Nikāyas and 0 in the seven Abhidhamma books, with 30 of its mula rows being the Visuddhimagga — I reproduced exactly by direct SQL (nik=0, abh=0, vism=30). The central interpretive readings are textually anchored: AN 3.86 literally carries "sīlesu paripūrakārī hoti samādhismiṃ mattaso kārī"; AN 9.36 runs the taint-destruction platform through the base of nothingness; the concentration counts (sukhino cittaṃ samādhiyati = 46, samādhiyati = 63, evaṃ samāhite citte = 58) match the study's own SAMADHI-COUNTS source. So no citation is misattributed and no "the canon says" rests on a lone passage that I could find. The real weaknesses are in the quantitative layer: a handful of round numbers are not cleanly traceable to a single defensible count, one Pali term is given a number that belongs to a looser stem, and the headline sabhāva figures are internally inconsistent across the study's own three working documents (§VII vs G1 vs DISCOVERY-PASS give three different pairs). These are precision/sourcing defects, not fabrications — but in a study whose whole rhetorical force is "every count is reproducible from the live database," an unreproducible or self-contradicting count is exactly the kind of crack that undermines the claim of auditability. Biggest concern: the sabhāva count inconsistency, because sabhāva is the keystone of the most ambitious general finding (G1, the substantialist-ontology drift).

findings.issue: The sabhāva counts that carry the study's most ambitious general claim (G1, the corpus-wide drift to own-nature language) are given three different ways across the study's own files. The readable §VII says sabhāva climbs 'to two thousand in the commentaries and over four thousand in the sub-commentaries' (≈2004/4406, matching HARDENING-CENSUS.md). G1 in the same readable paper says 'roughly 1,900 in the aṭṭhakathā and 3,950 in the ṭīkā' and its Where-line prints 'attha 1,906 / vism 553 / tika 3,951'. DISCOVERY-PASS.md gives yet a third pair, 'attha 1573 / vism 513 / tika 4018'. A reader cannot tell which is the real count, and §VII and G1 disagree with each other inside the single published document. I could not reconcile these against the live DB because the diacritic ILIKE scan over the 91,843 attha rows did not return under the corpus's concurrent load, but the inconsistency stands on the documents alone.
findings.where: §VII 'from a handful of canonical occurrences to two thousand in the commentaries and over four thousand in the sub-commentaries' vs G1 'roughly 1,900 in the aṭṭhakathā and 3,950 in the ṭīkā ... sabhāva layer split, canon ≈ 0 / attha 1,906 / vism 553 / tika 3,951'
findings.recommendation: Re-run one canonical sabhāva count (with the documented exclusions of purisabhāva and ekaṃsa-bhāvita), pick ONE attha/ṭīkā pair, and make §VII and G1 cite the same numbers. State whether the figure includes or excludes the Visuddhimagga rows, since vism is tagged work_role='mula' and a 'commentary' total that silently folds in 553 vism rows is itself a category choice that needs to be visible.

findings.issue: The readable paper attaches the number 'sixty-four rows' to the spelled-out compound cittekaggatā, but in the study's own SAMADHI-COUNTS.json the compound cittekaggata has only 5 canonical rows; the figure 64 is the count for the looser bare stem ekaggata (ekaggata.1canon = 64). So the sentence reads as though the specific compound 'one-pointedness' occurs 64 times in the canon, when the compound the reader sees in italics occurs 5 times and the 64 covers all -ekaggatā forms including cittassekaggatā, cittassa ekaggatā, etc. This over-states the canonical footprint of the precise term being italicised, in a passage whose whole point is that the closed-inventory naming is late.
findings.where: §VII 'one-pointedness (cittekaggatā), the depth-neutral genus of concentration, is canonical at sixty-four rows but is made a property of every mind-moment only in the commentary'
findings.recommendation: Either change the term to the stem ('the -ekaggatā family is canonical at sixty-four rows') or change the number to the compound's true canonical count (5), and say which lexical form the count is over. The argument survives either way, but the displayed word and the displayed number must match.

findings.issue: The 'about ten to one' ratio for jhānaṅga is not reproducible from the study's own ledger. HARDENING-CENSUS.md gives jhānaṅga as early 0, late 1, abh 12, para 1, comm 113, ṭīkā 211. Commentary-plus-ṭīkā (324) against canonical-plus-Abhidhamma scattered uses (~13) is roughly 25:1; commentary alone (113) against the ~1 early-canonical use is ~113:1. No grouping I can construct from the ledger yields 10:1. The number reads precise but is not traceable.
findings.where: §VII 'the collective term for them (jhānaṅga, "factor of absorption") are the analysis of the Abhidhamma and the commentary, where jhānaṅga outnumbers its scattered canonical uses about ten to one'
findings.recommendation: Replace 'about ten to one' with the actual grouping behind it (state numerator and denominator and which strata each pools), or soften to 'many times over'. As written it invites a reader to check it and find it does not hold.

findings.issue: The aṭṭhakathā count for ajjhāsaya is given three slightly different values: the readable §V says 'more than seven hundred times in the aṭṭhakathā'; HARDENING-CENSUS.md says 709; G4's Where-line says 'ajjhāsaya 4 canon / 701 attha'; DISCOVERY-PASS.md §3 says 'attha 680'. The four-Nikāya figure (4) is stable, but the commentary figure wanders by ~30 across the study's files. 'More than seven hundred' is safe against 701/709 but not against 680.
findings.where: §V 'The word ajjhāsaya, a person's bent that the teaching is fitted to, occurs four times in the four Nikāyas and then more than seven hundred times in the aṭṭhakathā' vs G4 'ajjhāsaya 4 canon / 701 attha'
findings.recommendation: Lock the aṭṭhakathā count to one re-run value and make §V, G4, and the ledger agree. If the spread is real (different stem boundaries), state the stem used.

findings.issue: The readable paper's central structural sentence — 'of the fifty-three structurally-canonical rows that hold the disambiguated temperament compound, thirty are the Visuddhimagga' — uses 'structurally-canonical' to mean work_role='mula', which in this corpus includes the Visuddhimagga (work_slug='pli-vism' is tagged mula, 1814 rows). I confirmed vism temperament rows = 30. But the published 263-instance census that backs the appendix tables contains 0 mula rows carrying the compound (all 28 compound-bearing census rows are attha/tika). So 'fifty-three structurally-canonical rows' is a raw-corpus SQL artifact in which 'canonical' is a structural tag the paper elsewhere spends paragraphs arguing is NOT chronologically canonical. The v2 rigor paper handles this carefully ('structurally mula-tagged but classical-commentary in stratum'); the readable paper compresses it to 'structurally-canonical', which a non-specialist will read as 'in the canon'.
findings.where: §V 'of the fifty-three structurally-canonical rows that hold the disambiguated temperament compound, thirty are the Visuddhimagga'
findings.recommendation: Add a half-clause making explicit that 'structurally-canonical' here means the mula tag and that the Visuddhimagga carries that tag despite being commentarial in stratum — otherwise the sentence appears to concede temperament compounds in the canon, contradicting the paper's own 0/0 headline three paragraphs earlier.

findings.issue: Two precise counts in the concentration section could not be reconciled to the study's own SAMADHI-COUNTS.json and I could not confirm them against the DB before the loaded corpus timed out my scans. (a) 'samādhisaṃvattanika ... ninety-two times over': the closest ledger entry is samvattanika.1canon = 89, a broader 'conducive-to' word, and the longer formula '...samadhisamvattanikehi' is 17 canon; no entry yields exactly 92. (b) 'access concentration ... 38 rows there [aṭṭhakathā], 39 in the Visuddhimagga': the ledger's upacarasamadhi is comm 77 / vism-not-separated, so the 38/39 split is not visible in the count source. The direction ('zero in the canon') is solid for both; only the exact figures are unsourced.
findings.where: §VII 'the noble ones' virtues are described, ninety-two times over, as samādhisaṃvattanika' and 'a commentarial coinage ... (38 rows there, 39 in the Visuddhimagga, against zero in the canon)'
findings.recommendation: Re-confirm both figures with the exact diacritic stem and stratum split used, and cite the stem. If 'ninety-two' is really the count of the bare samvattanika applied to the sīla-pericope, say so; if it is samādhisaṃvattanika specifically, the count needs to land near that rather than near 89.

findings.issue: G2 quotes the homophily formula as 'dhātuso sattā saṃsandanti samenti', but the actual SN 14.14 text reads 'Dhātusova, bhikkhave, sattā saṁsandanti samenti' (dhātuso + emphatic -va, with the vocative). The elision of 'bhikkhave' and the dropping of the enclitic -va are harmless for sense but the passage is presented as a verbatim Pali quotation in italics.
findings.where: Abstract/G2 'beings flock together and keep company (dhātuso sattā saṃsandanti samenti)' vs corpus 'Dhātusova, bhikkhave, sattā saṁsandanti samenti'
findings.recommendation: Either mark the quote as a normalized citation form or quote it as it stands in the row (dhātuso va ... saṃsandanti samenti). Minor, but the study elsewhere is strict about Pali display.

findings.issue: G2 is asserted as 'An early canonical law of assortative association' and 'a continuous law of how beings assort'. The mechanism rests on the SN 14 chapter (14.14/14.22/14.25, '17 canonical rows' all being the same repeated formula across one Saṃyutta). Calling a single repeated pericope from one chapter a 'law' is a stronger framing than one chapter's worth of attestation licenses, even though the count of 17 rows is real and I verified the three anchor suttas exist with the formula. This is the closest thing in the paper to a 'the canon says' resting on one locus.
findings.where: G2 'An early canonical law of assortative association ... It is asserted as a continuous law of how beings assort before any finite catalogue of person-types exists'
findings.recommendation: Down-grade 'law' to 'principle' or note explicitly that the attestation is one Saṃyutta chapter repeated, so the reader knows the breadth of warrant. The confidence:high tag is fine for existence; it is the word 'law' that over-reaches.

findings.issue: The Meghiya antidote claim is cited 'AN 9.3, with the Udāna doublet Ud 4.1 and the de-personalised AN 9.1'. The four-fold antidote set (asubha/mettā/ānāpānassati/anicca-saññā keyed to greed/hate/thinking/the conceit 'I am') is the AN 9.1 Sambodhi formulation; AN 9.3/Ud 4.1 (Meghiya) carry the same four developments but in the narrative-to-Meghiya frame. The readable attributes the full keyed list to 'Meghiya ... AN 9.3' as primary, whereas the v2 paper's own references treat AN 9.1 + Ud 4.1 as the seed rows. Not wrong (both suttas carry the four), but the primary attribution differs between the two papers of the same study.
findings.where: §II 'the Buddha gives four things to develop ... (AN 9.3, with the Udāna doublet Ud 4.1 and the de-personalised AN 9.1 ...)' vs FINDINGS-v2 references listing AN 9.1 and Ud 4.1 as 'the canonical seed'
findings.recommendation: Confirm the keyed antidote-to-defilement mapping is fully present in AN 9.3 (not only in AN 9.1) and align the primary citation between the readable and v2 papers so the seed sutta is the same in both.

reviews.overall: Read in full, the study is textually careful and its central empirical claim — that the six-temperament carita grid is absent from the four Nikāyas and the seven Abhidhamma books and crystallizes only at the para-canonical/commentarial hinge — is well-evidenced and, as far as it goes, defensible. The function-to-essence axis is a genuinely useful organizing frame, and the "describe-early, name-late" and sabhava-drift observations are real. But as a piece of comparative-Buddhist scholarship it has one disqualifying weakness for that genre: it engages essentially zero secondary literature. The only names in the document (Ñāṇamoli, B. C. Law, line 17) appear as translation cross-checks, not interlocutors. Every major thesis the study advances — late hardening of jhana analysis, the samatha/vipassana relation, the lower-fruit concentration floor, the rise of sabhava substantialism, the oral-transmission driver, the dry-insight figure as "later" — sits squarely on top of an existing, heavily-litigated scholarly debate (Cousins, Gombrich, Anālayo, Shankman, Brahmali, Gethin, Allon, Wynne, Vetter, Bronkhorst, Schmithausen) that the study neither cites, positions itself against, nor credits. The result is that several findings the paper presents with the cadence of discovery are in fact well-established positions, and at least one place ("the tradition" as a hardening monolith) edges toward straw-manning a position scholars have already nuanced. None of this means the textual work is wrong; it means the framing systematically overstates novelty and under-hedges against known counter-positions. Biggest concern: the paper must add a literature-engagement layer that (a) credits prior holders of its theses, (b) distinguishes what is novel (the corpus-wide stratified counts) from what is received, and (c) defends its positions against the named opponents rather than presenting them as fresh.

findings.issue: The study engages no secondary scholarship at all. The only proper names in the entire document are Ñāṇamoli and B. C. Law, and both appear solely as translation cross-checks ('checked against Ñāṇamoli for the Visuddhimagga and B. C. Law for the Puggalapaññatti'), not as scholarly positions to engage. For a paper whose theses live inside live debates owned by Cousins, Gombrich, Anālayo, Shankman, Brahmali, Gethin, Allon, and Wynne, the total absence of engagement is the central defect of the genre. A reader cannot tell what is the author's contribution versus received view.
findings.where: Method in brief, line 17: 'Renderings of commentary and Abhidhamma are the author's own gloss, checked against Ñāṇamoli for the Visuddhimagga and B. C. Law for the Puggalapaññatti.' — and nowhere else in the document.
findings.recommendation: Add a 'Relation to prior scholarship' section (or threaded citations) that names the major positions the paper builds on or argues against, and explicitly states what the stratified-count method adds that prior qualitative work did not. At minimum: Cousins/Gombrich/Vetter/Bronkhorst on samatha vs. vipassana and the place of jhana; Shankman and Brahmali on the jhana controversy and dry-insight; Anālayo on the early-discourse register and the Niddesa/commentarial layer; Gethin on Abhidhamma and the path; Allon/Wynne on oral transmission. Without this layer the paper is not submittable as comparative scholarship.

findings.issue: The thesis that the late commentary 'freezes' a situational/yoked samatha-vipassana relation into fixed dry-insight (sukkhavipassaka) and calm/insight 'vehicles' is presented as the study's own reading, but this is one of the most-worked claims in the field. Cousins ('Samatha-yana and Vipassana-yana', 1984) is precisely the foundational treatment of these vehicle terms; Gombrich (How Buddhism Began) and Vetter (Ideas and Meditative Practices of Early Buddhism) argued the two-strand thesis decades ago; Brahmali and Shankman have litigated the dry-insight/jhana question at length. The paper's 'these are zero in the canon and only in the commentary, the same late signature as carita' is a real datum, but the interpretive claim it supports is received, not new.
findings.where: Section VII, 'Calm, insight...': 'The commentary is what freezes it, into the calm-vehicle and insight-vehicle practitioner and the dry-insight worker (samathayanika, vipassanayanika, sukkhavipassaka). These are zero in the canon and only in the commentary, the same late signature as carita.'
findings.recommendation: Cite Cousins 1984 by name for the vehicle terminology and its lateness, and Gombrich/Vetter for the two-strand reading; then frame the contribution as 'a quantified stratal confirmation of a position previously argued qualitatively' rather than as a fresh finding. Note where the count strengthens or qualifies the prior claim.

findings.issue: The lower-fruit concentration argument takes a definite side in the single most disputed question in modern practice-scholarship (does stream-entry require jhana?) and even flags it as 'among the most disputed in modern practice-scholarship', yet cites none of the disputants. Brahmali's 'Jhana and Lokuttarajjhana' and Shankman's 'The Experience of Samadhi' are the central modern texts on exactly this; the samahite citte / sukhino cittam samadhiyati causal-fruit argument and the Susima a-fortiori are standard moves in that literature. Presenting them as the study's structural 'three facts' without acknowledging that these are contested, much-rehearsed arguments overstates originality and hides the strongest opposing reading (that 'right concentration = the four jhanas' formula is definitional and the lists are not exhaustive).
findings.where: Section VII, 'How much concentration the lower path requires': 'One question sits next to this one and is among the most disputed in modern practice-scholarship... three structural facts pull the floor for stream-entry and once-return below full absorption.'
findings.recommendation: Name Brahmali and Shankman (and the wider Sujato/Brahmali vs. dry-insight-defenders exchange) where this question is raised; present the 'three structural facts' as the author's marshalling of evidence within an existing debate, and steelman the maximalist counter (sammasamadhi-as-jhana definitional reading) more than the single concessive sentence currently given. The paper already concedes 'the maximalist side has real evidence' — but it should attribute that side to its actual proponents.

findings.issue: G1 frames the sabhava drift as 'a datum for the long-running scholarly question of when Theravada acquired a substantialist ontology' but supplies the datum as if the question were merely open and the count fresh. The rise of sabhava and the charge of commentarial/Abhidhamma substantialism is extensively treated by Karunadasa (The Theravada Abhidhamma), Ronkin (Early Buddhist Metaphysics, whose entire book is about the dhamma-theory and sabhava), and Gethin. Presenting a raw layer-count without engaging Ronkin/Karunadasa, who have already mapped this terrain conceptually, makes the finding look more isolated and more novel than it is.
findings.where: Findings of general importance, G1: 'This is a datum for the long-running scholarly question of when Theravada acquired a substantialist ontology. Where: sabhava layer split, canon ≈ 0 / attha 1,906 / vism 553 / tika 3,951.'
findings.recommendation: Cite Ronkin (Early Buddhist Metaphysics) and Karunadasa on the sabhava/dhamma-theory question; frame G1's contribution as the quantitative stratal gradient (canon→attha→tika peak), which is the genuinely new part, while crediting the conceptual question to existing scholarship.

findings.issue: The framing of 'the tradition' hardening an adaptive teaching into closed systems risks straw-manning, because it treats systematization as a one-directional defect of 'a thousand years of Abhidhamma, commentary, and sub-commentary' without engaging scholarship that reads the Abhidhamma project sympathetically as legitimate doctrinal development (Gethin, 'The Mātikās'; Ronkin; Cox). The study does partially self-correct (the 'addition' finding, the regression-of-confidence finding, the counter-cases), which is to its credit, but the abstract and conclusion still cast the movement in a register ('fix that supple teaching into closed systems') that a sympathetic reader of the Abhidhamma would call tendentious. The texts-noticing-the-tension move ('A teaching of non-self... comes to rest on the language of own-nature') is rhetorically strong but is an interpretive provocation that needs a counter-voice.
findings.where: Abstract, line 7: 'The thousand years of Abhidhamma, commentary, and sub-commentary that follow fix that supple teaching into closed systems: standing types and named tiers, closed lists and measured quantities.' And conclusion, line 13: 'A teaching of non-self and impermanence comes, in its later scholarship, to rest on the language of own-nature and fixed measure.'
findings.recommendation: Hedge the value-laden framing and engage the sympathetic reading of Abhidhamma systematization (Gethin on matikas as exegetical method; Cox/Ronkin on the dhamma-theory as a non-substantialist analytic). State explicitly that 'hardening' is the author's descriptive axis and not a verdict that the development is illegitimate, and acknowledge that the commentators would not recognize own-nature talk as a betrayal of anatta (the Theravada self-understanding is that sabhava is precisely not atta).

findings.issue: The conclusion raises the oral-transmission hypothesis as a possible driver of systematization and even gives the mechanism ('a teaching must be formulaic and listed to survive memorization at all'), which is exactly the thesis of Allon (Style and Function; and his work on mnemonic features) and Wynne ('The Oral Transmission of the Early Buddhist Literature'), plus Cousins ('Pali Oral Literature'). The study presents this as an open conjecture it cannot settle, but the formulaic-for-memorization claim is not the author's speculation — it is an established scholarly position with named owners and evidence. Leaving it uncited makes a borrowed thesis look like an offhand guess.
findings.where: Section VIII, line 97: 'It is possible that an even simpler teaching was already being systematized in the centuries of oral transmission before writing, for the plain reason that a teaching must be formulaic and listed to survive memorization at all.'
findings.recommendation: Cite Allon, Wynne, and Cousins on oral transmission and mnemonic formulae. The honest move is to say the oral-systematization layer is well-attested by this scholarship (so the 'earliest recoverable layer is already systematized' caveat is not merely possible but argued in the literature), which actually strengthens the study's own limit-statement.

findings.issue: The claim that the five-factor jhananga analysis and the term jhananga are the Abhidhamma's/commentary's work, absent from the Nikaya first-jhana formula, is presented as a stratal discovery. This is well-known: the move from the canonical savitakkam-savicaram description to the five-factor list (adding ekaggata) is a standard observation in the jhana literature (Cousins, Gethin's The Buddhist Path to Awakening on the jhana factors, Stuart-Fox 'Jhana and Buddhist Scholasticism', Bucknell). The count (jhananga ~10:1 attha-over-canon) is new and valuable; the qualitative claim is not.
findings.where: Section VII, 'Calm, insight...': 'The familiar count of five jhana factors, which adds one-pointedness (cittekaggata) as a fifth, and the collective term for them (jhananga, "factor of absorption") are the analysis of the Abhidhamma and the commentary, where jhananga outnumbers its scattered canonical uses about ten to one.'
findings.recommendation: Cite the existing scholarship on the jhana-factor analysis (e.g. Stuart-Fox 1989; Gethin 2001; Cousins) and frame the 10:1 ratio as quantitative confirmation. Flag that ekaggata's presence in the first jhana is itself debated (some argue it is implied), so 'adds one-pointedness as a fifth' should hedge against the reading that one-pointedness was always implicit.

findings.issue: The claim that upacara/appana/parikamma as concentration grades and khanika-samadhi are entirely commentarial coinages, zero in canon, is correct and well-supported by the counts, but it is also a known result (Cousins 'Buddhist Jhana', Gethin, and the standard handbooks treat access/absorption as Visuddhimagga-stage developments; momentary concentration's lateness is discussed in the Mahasi-vs-jhana debates). Presented here as if first established.
findings.where: Section VII, 'Calm, insight...': 'The familiar ladder of preparatory, access, and full concentration (parikamma, upacara, appana) is entirely the commentary's: each of the three is attested zero times as a grade of concentration in the four Nikayas and the seven Abhidhamma books, and so is momentary concentration (khanika-samadhi).'
findings.recommendation: Add a brief citation crediting the prior recognition of these terms' commentarial origin; keep the zero-count as the study's precise contribution. This is lower severity because the empirical claim is solid and the term is genuinely commentarial — the issue is only attribution of the qualitative point.

findings.issue: The study's entire chronology rests on a layered stratigraphy (four Nikayas early; Niddesa/Patisambhidamagga 'after the four-Nikaya prose'; Abhidhamma; commentary; sub-commentary) presented as settled, but relative dating of the Khuddaka texts and the question of whether all four Nikayas are uniformly 'early' is itself contested scholarship (von Hinuber's Handbook of Pali Literature, Norman, Anālayo's comparative-Agama work showing late strata inside the Nikayas). The paper asserts 'a layer the field places after the four-Nikaya prose' without citing who places it there or acknowledging dissent.
findings.where: Section IV, line 35: 'it sits in a layer the field places after the four-Nikaya prose.' And the abstract's six-strata scheme generally.
findings.recommendation: Cite von Hinuber and/or Norman for the relative dating of the Niddesa and Patisambhidamagga, and add one sentence acknowledging that 'early' is itself a contested stratification and that some Nikaya material is demonstrably late (Anālayo's comparative work). This protects the chronology claims that the whole argument depends on.

findings.issue: G3 (the diacritic-and-substring search trap) is presented as a reusable methodological finding 'of general importance', but a diacritic-insensitive substring search over-/under-recalling in a morphologically rich language is a basic and well-understood NLP/digital-humanities point, not a finding of general scholarly importance. Elevating it to a numbered general finding alongside genuinely substantive results (G1 sabhava drift, G2 assortative association) inflates its standing.
findings.where: Findings of general importance, G3: 'A reusable diacritic-and-substring trap in corpus search. A diacritic-naive substring search miscounts in both directions, and this study is a clean demonstration.'
findings.recommendation: Either demote G3 to a methods/limitations note rather than a 'finding of general importance', or keep it but frame it as a worked corpus-philology cautionary example rather than a novel result. The carita-specific instance is a nice illustration; the general lesson is not new.

findings.issue: The reading of the canonical kasina as 'a measureless perception (appamana, a refusal of measure rather than a claim of the infinite)' that 'becomes a manufactured disc' in the commentary is interpretively strong and contestable. The canonical kasina material (e.g. the kasinayatanas in AN/DN) is terse and its early practice-content is genuinely uncertain; reading appamana as a deliberate 'refusal of measure' rather than simply 'boundless/immeasurable' imports an interpretive thesis. This is the kind of claim that needs a hedge and a citation (Gethin on the kasinas, or the relevant Vism scholarship).
findings.where: Abstract, line 11, and Section VII, line 63: 'The kasina that the canon offers as a measureless perception (appamana, a refusal of measure rather than a claim of the infinite) becomes a manufactured disc in the commentary.'
findings.recommendation: Hedge 'a refusal of measure rather than a claim of the infinite' as the author's reading and cite at least one prior treatment of the kasina's early-vs-commentarial content (Gethin; or note the scarcity of canonical detail explicitly). As written it asserts a debatable semantic gloss of appamana as settled.

findings.issue: The gotrabhu ('change-of-lineage') claim — 'barely present in the suttas and becomes a fixture of the Abhidhamma' (6 canon vs 200) — brushes the long debate over the term's early meaning and provenance (it appears in the Theragatha/Majjhima and the Gotrabhu-sutta of AN; its technical path-moment sense is indeed later). This is contested ground (von Hinuber and others on gotrabhu); presenting the count without the interpretive caveat that the canonical occurrences may carry a different sense is the same over-recall trap the study itself warns about in G3.
findings.where: Section VII, 'Where the hardening happens': 'The change-of-lineage moment (gotrabhu) is barely present in the suttas and becomes a fixture of the Abhidhamma's analysis of the mind, at two hundred occurrences there against six in the early canon.'
findings.recommendation: Confirm that the 6 canonical gotrabhu rows actually carry (or do not carry) the technical change-of-lineage sense, applying the study's own sense-audit standard, and add a citation/footnote on the term's disputed early meaning. State whether the 6:200 ratio is a meaning-shift or merely a frequency shift.

findings.issue: The cosmology strand (cakkavala, kappavinasa, yojana-counted realms as commentarial vs. a canon that 'defines nibbana only in the negative') is presented as a parallel discovery, but the lateness and elaboration of Buddhist cosmology relative to the suttas is thoroughly established (Gethin 'Cosmology and Meditation'; Kloetzli; Sadakata). The negative-only definition of nibbana and the world-system's commentarial inflation are textbook points.
findings.where: Section VII, 'Where the hardening happens', line 63: 'The bounded world-sphere (cakkavala), almost absent from the canon, becomes a commentarial fixture at over five hundred occurrences. The cycle by which a world is destroyed and reformed (kappavinasa) is absent from the entire canon and is built in the commentaries.'
findings.recommendation: Cite Gethin ('Cosmology and Meditation', JIABS) and the standard cosmology scholarship; frame the counts as quantitative support for an established picture rather than as new terrain.

findings.issue: The paper's strongest defense against the straw-man and novelty charges — the reflexive 'to trace a tradition's hardening is itself an act of systematization' paragraph — is genuinely good but is buried at the very end of Section VIII and is not connected to any actual scholarly interlocutor. Its honesty is asserted rather than demonstrated through engagement with someone who would push back.
findings.where: Section VIII, line 99: 'To trace a tradition's hardening into fixed systems is itself an act of systematization. It reduces a living and many-voiced literature to counts, to axes, and to named jumps, and there is no neutral place to stand.'
findings.recommendation: Keep this paragraph but earn it: pair the reflexive caveat with at least one concrete scholarly position the study could be wrong against, so the humility is operational rather than rhetorical.

reviews.lens: Prose and style critic applying house editorial gates (em-dash, first person, banned words, punchline cadence, over-parallel triads, inflated diction, hollow openers, comma-splice/appositive overuse; plus readability and rhythm)
reviews.overall: The hard gates pass cleanly: zero em-dashes or en-dashes, no authorial first person (the one "I" is inside a quoted canonical refrain, "the conceit 'I am'"), and neither banned phrase (load-bearing, cross-cutting) appears. The diction is mostly plain and the citation discipline is genuinely strong. The prose is, however, far from neutral on rhythm: it leans hard on one device — the clipped parallel sentence run, often anaphoric — and on the setup-and-payoff "punchline" cadence that the house standard names as an AI-tell. These cluster in the high-visibility frames (the Background, the Conclusion, the §VII enumerations, and G2) where they do the most damage, because the reader meets the machinery before the evidence. The signature is so consistent that the writing starts to feel manufactured in the same way the study accuses the commentaries of being. None of it is unsupported scholarship; it is a rhythm problem and a small number of overstated closers. Biggest concern: the abstract and conclusion are built almost entirely out of two-beat and three-beat parallel sentences plus aphoristic closers, and that cadence should be broken up before this reads as polished rather than thought.

findings.issue: The Background opens with a stack of clipped parallel sentences, including a three-beat anaphoric run on 'They': two register-sentences ('teaches in one register' / 'teaches in another') followed by 'They watch the root now active in a mind. They weigh how far a person's faculties have ripened. They place the whole world inside the fathom-long body.' This is exactly the over-parallel triad the house standard flags, and it is the first thing the reader meets.
findings.where: Abstract, Background: 'They watch the root now active in a mind. They weigh how far a person's faculties have ripened. They place the whole world inside the fathom-long body.'
findings.recommendation: Break the anaphora. Fold at least one of the three 'They' sentences into a subordinate clause or vary the subject, and merge the two opening 'register' sentences so the paragraph does not open on four consecutive short parallel beats.

findings.issue: The Conclusion delivers the thesis as a pair of aphoristic two-clause payoff sentences with identical architecture: 'A present function is set into a fixed essence. An open and measureless formulation is closed into a concrete and bounded one.' This is the named AI-tell — the setup-and-payoff sentence — doubled, and removing em-dashes (already done) does not defuse it. The matched abstract/concrete balance ('fixed essence' / 'concrete and bounded one') makes it read as composed rather than reasoned.
findings.where: Abstract, Conclusion: 'A present function is set into a fixed essence. An open and measureless formulation is closed into a concrete and bounded one.'
findings.recommendation: Rewrite at least one of the two as ordinary expository prose tied to a specific instance rather than a polished maxim. Let the example (carita, the kasiṇa) carry the generalization instead of restating it as an epigram.

findings.issue: G2 runs five consecutive sentences each beginning 'It is...' / 'It has...': 'It is an explicit homophily principle... It is asserted as a continuous law... It has nothing to do with matching a practice... it is a canonical sociology... It is of interest to anyone...'. The monotone 'It is' anaphora reads as a generated list of assertions rather than an argument and flattens the rhythm of an otherwise interesting finding.
findings.where: Findings of general importance, G2: 'It is an explicit homophily principle with a named mechanism... It is asserted as a continuous law... It has nothing to do with matching a practice to a person... It is of interest to anyone studying...'
findings.recommendation: Collapse the run. Combine the homophily-principle and continuous-law claims into one sentence, and cut or subordinate 'It is of interest to anyone studying how a tradition theorizes social grouping,' which is a hollow significance-claim rather than a finding.

findings.issue: Section closers land on short aphoristic one-liners that read as drumbeats: 'The latest layer hardens the hardest.' (§VII), 'What it never does is fix how much less.' (§VII calm/insight), 'So is the cosmos it sits within.' (abstract Conclusion). Each is a deliberate mic-drop. One such closer is fine; the pattern of ending unit after unit on a terse payoff is the cadence the standard warns against.
findings.where: §VII 'The latest layer hardens the hardest.'; §VII 'What it never does is fix how much less.'; Abstract 'So is the cosmos it sits within.'
findings.recommendation: Keep at most one of these as a deliberate close; rework the others into the body of the preceding sentence so the section ends on substance rather than on a slogan. 'The latest layer hardens the hardest' in particular trades precision for assonance.

findings.category: parallel enumerated frame repeated across sections
findings.issue: The 'The first is... The second is... The third is...' enumerated frame is used twice in close succession with near-identical phrasing (§VII 'The first is a divergence. The second is an addition. The third is a regression...' and §VII.3 'The first is what the lists... The second is that... The third is an argument...'), and a third truncated instance opens the limits passage ('The first is when the systematizing began'). The repeated scaffold makes consecutive sections feel templated.
findings.where: §VII: 'The first is a divergence. The second is an addition. The third is a regression of confidence...'; and the lower-path section: 'The first is what the lists of stream-entry leave out... The second is... The third is an argument from the ceiling...'
findings.recommendation: Vary the connective scaffolding between the two enumerations so they do not read as the same template instantiated twice — e.g. let the second section introduce its three structural facts without the 'The first/second/third is' frame.

findings.issue: Several sentences announce their own rhetorical move rather than just making it: 'This is the birth certificate of the apparatus' (§IV), 'The story is the epistemic point in narrative form' (§V), 'the exceptions are the divergence, the addition, and the regression seen from the side of the count' (§VII). These are clever framings that call attention to the writing; the 'birth certificate' metaphor in particular is a flourish in an otherwise austere academic register.
findings.where: §IV: 'This is the birth certificate of the apparatus, ascribed to the Buddha's knowing'; §V: 'The story is the epistemic point in narrative form.'
findings.recommendation: Trim the metaphors to plain statement. 'birth certificate of the apparatus' can become 'the earliest attestation of the apparatus'; 'the epistemic point in narrative form' restates what the paragraph already showed and can be cut.

findings.issue: Several long sentences join independent clauses or pile loose appositive phrases with commas where a semicolon or full stop is needed, hurting readability. E.g. §II qualification two is a single ~70-word sentence chaining 'are in the canon mostly fixed doctrinal lists, the seven latent defilements every unawakened being carries and the psychic-power item..., not ways of telling one person's standing type from another's, and are not counted here as individuation.' The appositive 'the seven latent defilements... and the psychic-power item...' is spliced in by comma and overloads the clause.
findings.where: §II: 'two terms that look like early "disposition" vocabulary, *anusaya* (latent tendency) and *cetopariya*..., are in the canon mostly fixed doctrinal lists, the seven latent defilements every unawakened being carries and the psychic-power item of knowing a mind's present state, not ways of telling...'
findings.recommendation: Split this into two sentences and set the gloss off with a semicolon or em-dash-free dash equivalent (colon), e.g. end one clause at 'fixed doctrinal lists' then start a new sentence naming the two. Apply the same to the §83 chain on caused concentration.

findings.issue: Inline glosses set off by commas stack densely enough in places to obscure the main clause, especially in §II and §VII. §II's seven-noble-persons sentence is a single clause carrying six parenthetical Pali-plus-gloss appositives in a row; readable in a list but heavy as running prose. §VII.regression has a deeply nested appositive: 'the canon's own faculty for reading persons, the maturity of another's faculties and the knowledge of their dispositions, is staked under a knowing-formula...'.
findings.where: §VII regression sentence: 'the canon's own faculty for reading persons, the maturity of another's faculties and the knowledge of their dispositions, is staked under a knowing-formula the temperament grid never claims.'
findings.recommendation: Where three or more appositive glosses stack in one sentence, convert to a brief enumerated list or split the sentence so the subject and verb are not separated by a multi-item parenthetical. This is a readability fix, not a content change.

findings.issue: A few word choices reach for effect: 'the design vocabulary blooms' / 'a whole design vocabulary blooms' (used twice, §V and §I summary), 'the language of own-nature reaches its peak,' and 'the latest layer hardens the hardest.' 'Blooms' is repeated as a signature verb and lightly anthropomorphizes; the assonant 'hardens the hardest' privileges sound over precision.
findings.where: §V heading and body: 'the design vocabulary blooms' / 'a whole design vocabulary blooms'; §VII: 'The latest layer hardens the hardest.'
findings.recommendation: Use 'blooms' once at most; replace the second with a plain verb ('appears,' 'proliferates'). Replace 'hardens the hardest' with a precise statement ('the sub-commentaries harden the vocabulary furthest').

findings.issue: A couple of paragraph and clause openers add cadence without content: 'And in the same breath the manual that fixes the grid declines to certify its most detailed part' (§V) uses 'in the same breath' as a rhetorical hinge for two separate textual facts; 'Three things follow when the whole field is read at once' (§VII) is a serviceable but slightly grand announcement of a list.
findings.where: §V: 'And in the same breath the manual that fixes the grid declines to certify its most detailed part.'
findings.recommendation: Drop 'in the same breath' (the two Visuddhimagga facts are not literally adjacent) in favor of a neutral connective such as 'The same manual then declines to certify its most detailed part.'

reviews.lens: General scholarly reader judging significance, clarity, and rigor (comparative Buddhist studies)
reviews.overall: This is a genuinely strong, ambitious piece. Its core thesis — that the systematization of early Buddhist teaching is gradual and multi-staged rather than a single canon/commentary break, and that it takes the recurrent form of a present function being frozen into a fixed essence, in two registers (persons and cosmos) — is non-obvious, well-supported by reproducible counts, and reflexively honest about its own limits (the closing MN 22 "raft" turn on the study itself is excellent). The carita zero-attestation is a real, hard finding, and the discovery that a carita-shaped search misses the seven-person liberation roster is a sharp methodological lesson. So the study is not thin or obvious. But it has real weaknesses a tough reviewer must flag. (1) The single biggest gap: the central function-vs-essence axis is asserted as "the most defensible description" but is never tested against a competing reading of the same data (e.g. that what looks like "essentializing" is just normal scholastic disambiguation/pedagogy, or that the count growth merely tracks corpus volume growth). Without a control for sheer text-mass and without engaging an alternative interpretation, the headline claim rests on selected exemplars. (2) Counting methodology is under-specified in the readable paper itself: raw occurrence counts of sabhava, ajjhasaya, etc. are compared across strata of wildly different size with no normalization, no denominators, and no per-stratum word-totals — a fatal ambiguity for any quantitative claim ("climbs at every step"). (3) Engagement with prior scholarship is essentially absent: the dry-insight/jhana debate, the gradual-vs-sudden systematization question, and the substantialist-ontology question all have large literatures (Cousins, Gethin, Gombrich, Anālayo, Ronkin on sabhava) and none is cited or even named, which a comparative-studies reader will find disqualifying for a "publishable standard" claim. (4) The abstract is long, eloquent, but not fully self-contained: it never states what counts as the unit, never gives a single concrete number, and leans on phrasings ("the move is real") that assume the body. Fix these and the paper lands; as written it is persuasive essayistic synthesis more than demonstrated result.

findings.category: Untested central claim / missing rival hypothesis
findings.issue: The load-bearing function-to-essence axis is offered as the best description but is never weighed against an obvious competing reading — that later strata are not 'essentializing' so much as doing ordinary scholastic disambiguation, pedagogy, and definitional housekeeping, which any maturing technical literature accrues. The paper concedes 'It is offered as the most defensible description of the field, not as a proof,' but a peer reviewer needs to see the rival entertained and argued down, not just the favored axis asserted.
findings.where: VIII / limits: 'The function-versus-essence reading is the axis the data cleaves on most sharply. It is offered as the most defensible description of the field, not as a proof.'
findings.recommendation: Add a short subsection that states at least one serious alternative interpretation (e.g. 'scholastic precisification, not essentialization' or 'mnemonic-to-analytic shift') and shows on the same counts why function-to-essence fits better — or concede it is one defensible reading among several and soften the conclusion accordingly.

findings.issue: Every cross-stratum claim ('climbs at every step', 'over five hundred occurrences', 'two thousand in the commentaries and over four thousand in the sub-commentaries', 'about ten to one') compares raw occurrence counts across strata of very different total size. The commentaries and sub-commentaries are vastly larger than the four Nikayas (the CLAUDE.md corpus itself records ~93k attha + ~78k tika paragraph rows vs ~14k mula). A term rising from canon to tika could rise simply because there is 10x more text. The readable paper gives no denominators, no per-million-word rates, and no acknowledgement of this confound.
findings.where: VII / 'sabhava ... climbs at every step, from a handful of canonical occurrences to two thousand in the commentaries and over four thousand in the sub-commentaries'; G1 'roughly 1,900 in the attha and 3,950 in the tika'.
findings.recommendation: Report at least one normalized figure (occurrences per N rows or per million tokens) for the headline terms, or explicitly state that raw counts are used and that the qualitative pattern (zero-vs-present) not the magnitude is what is claimed. The zero-attestation findings (carita, upacara-as-tier, cakkavala) survive this; the 'intensifies/peaks' magnitude claims do not without it.

findings.issue: For a study claiming publishable rigor on heavily-worked questions, there is no engagement with the existing literature. The dry-insight / jhana-requirement debate (Cousins, Gethin, Gombrich, Anālayo, Brahm vs Sujato), the gradual-systematization question, and the substantialist-ontology question for sabhava (Ronkin's 'Early Buddhist Metaphysics' is the standard reference) are all named-but-uncited. G1 even gestures at 'the long-running scholarly question of when Theravada acquired a substantialist ontology' without a single citation. A comparative-Buddhist-studies reader will read this as reinventing known debates.
findings.where: VII 'the most-disputed corner of this material'; VIII 'among the most disputed in modern practice-scholarship'; G1 'a datum for the long-running scholarly question of when Theravada acquired a substantialist ontology'.
findings.recommendation: Add a brief positioning paragraph (or footnotes) citing the principal prior positions on each disputed point and stating what this study adds to them (the reproducible cross-stratum count is the genuine contribution — say so against named prior work, especially Ronkin on sabhava).

findings.issue: The abstract is long and rhetorically polished but not self-contained in the way an abstract must be. It never names the unit of analysis (the textual row), never gives a single concrete number (not even the headline carita=0), and relies on assertions that only the body justifies ('The move is real', 'the counts are as clean'). A reader who reads only the abstract cannot tell what was counted or how strong the evidence is.
findings.where: Abstract, Results: 'The move is real. It takes one form, and it recurs at more than one jump.' and Methods: 'Every count comes from a query grouped on textual stratum against the live corpus.'
findings.recommendation: Add one or two anchoring numbers to the abstract (e.g. carita-temperament compound = 0 across the four Nikayas and seven Abhidhamma books; sabhava near-zero in canon vs thousands in commentary) and one clause naming the unit (passage/row counts grouped by stratum). Trim one of the parallel rhetorical sentences to make room.

findings.category: Scope inflation / two papers in one
findings.issue: Sections VII.2 and VII.3 ('Calm, insight, and how much concentration the path asks' and 'How much concentration the lower path requires') together run roughly a third of the paper and constitute a substantial independent argument about the jhana-requirement debate, with its own three-pronged structural case (negative space, caused-fruit, argument-from-ceiling). This is a second study grafted onto the first. It is excellent material, but it overwhelms the stated subject (function-to-essence systematization) and the concentration-floor conclusion is not actually a function-to-essence finding — it is a doctrinal-underdetermination finding. The reader loses the through-line.
findings.where: Section VII subsections 'Calm, insight, and how much concentration the path asks' and 'How much concentration the lower path requires' (lines 67-87).
findings.recommendation: Either explicitly subordinate the concentration-floor argument as one worked exhibit of the general pattern (and cut it by half), or split it into a companion study and reference it. As is, the paper's center of gravity drifts away from its title.

findings.issue: Several quantitative claims in the prose lack the inline grounding that other claims have, so a reader cannot check them against the appendix. 'of the fifty-three structurally-canonical rows ... thirty are the Visuddhimagga' and 'the technical sense of kammatthana ... 134 of 148 rows' are asserted without a 'Where' pointer, unlike the G-findings which carry explicit count strings. 'Of the two dozen or so transitions counted this way' is vague ('or so') for a quantitative backbone claim.
findings.where: V: 'of the fifty-three structurally-canonical rows that hold the disambiguated temperament compound, thirty are the Visuddhimagga ... (134 of 148 rows)'; VII: 'Of the two dozen or so transitions counted this way'.
findings.recommendation: Pin the two-dozen transitions to an actual enumerated table (or say 'N=24' and point to it), and add a brief source pointer for the 53/30 and 134/148 figures so they are auditable like the rest.

findings.category: Definitional gradient flagged but not resolved
findings.issue: The paper repeatedly notes that its key gradient may be 'partly-definitional' (coding chronological stratum can re-split the canon by the very feature being measured), and the memory/method notes acknowledge this, but the readable paper does not surface the circularity risk to the reader: if 'late' rows are partly identified by how essentializing they are, then 'essentializing increases with lateness' is partly built in. This is the most serious validity threat to the central claim and it is under-disclosed in the body.
findings.where: Abstract: 'gradient flagged partly-definitional' does NOT appear in the readable paper — the readable version states the gradient as clean ('the counts are as clean') without the circularity caveat that the underlying dataset carries.
findings.recommendation: Add one sentence in VII or VIII disclosing that textual-stratum assignment is partly independent of and partly entangled with the feature being measured, and state how the coding kept them apart (independent stratum coding before count, per the method) so the reader can judge the residual circularity.

findings.issue: A few formulations assert more confidence than the evidence licenses or editorialize in a way that pre-judges the reader. 'The texts themselves would notice the tension here' personifies the corpus to score the central irony; 'The latest layer hardens the hardest' is a catchy line resting on un-normalized counts (see the normalization finding); 'and they are not few' about the counter-cases is asserted without a number where one is available.
findings.where: Abstract/VIII 'The texts themselves would notice the tension'; VII 'The latest layer hardens the hardest'; VII 'A clear minority run against the trend, and they are not few.'
