findings.recommendation: Replace the personification with a direct statement (the later scholarship rests a non-self/impermanence teaching on own-nature language — a tension internal to the tradition), and give the counter-case minority an actual count rather than 'not few'.

findings.issue: The method note states 'Renderings of commentary and Abhidhamma are the author's own gloss, checked against Nanamoli for the Visuddhimagga and B. C. Law for the Puggalapannatti.' For the vivid sub-commentary exhibit (heart-blood colours keyed to temperament, Vibh-a) and the goldsmith's-son narrative (Dhp-a), which carry real interpretive weight, no external check is named, and these are exactly the passages a skeptical reader will want corroborated before accepting the 'physiology' claim.
findings.where: VI: 'the blood of a greed-type runs red, of a hate-type dark, of a delusion-type the colour of water in which meat has been washed (Vibh-a §70.58)'; method note 'checked against Nanamoli ... and B. C. Law'.
findings.recommendation: Either extend the corroboration statement to cover the tika/Dhp-a exhibits (or note they are uncorroborated author glosses) so the reader knows which renderings are externally checked and which are not.

findings.category: Cosmology domain thinly evidenced relative to its billing
findings.issue: The 'description of the world' register is presented as a co-equal second domain proving the pattern is general, but it is supported by markedly fewer worked exemplars than the persons domain (kasina/appamana, cakkavala, kappavinasa, yojana/Meru, nibbana-as-non-place) and several are single-term counts rather than read-on-sampled-rows analyses. The strong parallelism claim ('the description of the world follows the same line, and the counts are as clean') leans on the persons-domain rigor being assumed to carry over.
findings.where: VII: 'The description of the world follows the same line, and the counts are as clean.'
findings.recommendation: Either deepen at least one cosmology exemplar to the sense-audited standard of the persons domain (appamana-kasina is the best candidate), or downgrade the parallelism claim to 'the same direction, on a thinner sample' so the two domains are not presented as equally demonstrated.

findings.issue: G1-G4 are valuable but G3 (the diacritic/substring search trap) is a methodological lesson about doing corpus research, not a finding about Buddhism, and sits oddly in a 'findings of general importance' list addressed to comparative-Buddhist-studies readers alongside G2 (a substantive canonical sociology finding). Mixing a tooling caveat with substantive doctrinal findings dilutes both.
findings.where: Findings of general importance: 'G3. A reusable diacritic-and-substring trap in corpus search.'
findings.recommendation: Consider moving G3 into the Method/Limits apparatus (it is a method lesson) and keeping the general-importance section for substantive findings (G1, G2, G4), or label G3 explicitly as a methodological note.

findings.issue: A few citations are loose enough that a checker would stall. 'over a hundred canonical rows' for ditthadhammasukhavihara, 'some two hundred canonical rows' for the first-jhana formula, and 'ninety-two times over' for samadhisamvattanika are given as round prose figures without the exact-row-string format used elsewhere; and 'PP 1.3' / 'PS 3.5' / 'CND 19' use abbreviations not all expanded at first use for a general reader.
findings.where: VII: 'set out in some two hundred canonical rows'; 'described, ninety-two times over, as samadhisamvattanika'; IV 'PS 3.5'; IV 'CND 19'.
findings.recommendation: Standardize the count format (give exact row counts as in the G-findings) and expand abbreviations on first use (Cula-Niddesa = CND, Patisambhidamagga = PS) for a non-specialist scholarly reader.

reviews.lens: Independent peer review focused on out-of-scope issues, data gaps, and claims needing their own study (cosmology census flagged spot-check-only). Verified against the live dhamma-pg corpus, the HARDENING-CENSUS, DISCOVERY-PASS, FINDINGS-v2, and the build_dataset gating.
reviews.overall: The person-guidance and samādhi spine of this paper is genuinely strong: the headline (the temperament compound is a hard 0 in the four Nikāyas and the seven Abhidhamma books, fixed only later) is gated in build_dataset.py, re-verified in DISCOVERY-PASS against live rows, and the rival-vocabulary recall (the seven ariya-puggala roster, the four learners, faculty-maturity) genuinely repairs the original carita-only blind spot. The "function-to-essence x stratum" axis is defensible and the counter-cases are counted, not waved away. The serious weakness sits in the SECOND domain the paper now advertises in its title and abstract: the "description of the world." That half (cakkavāḷa, kappavināsa, appamāṇa-kasiṇa, yojana-counting, nibbāna-as-not-a-place, Mount Meru, world-ages) is NOT in the HARDENING-CENSUS table of 27 transitions, NOT in FINDINGS-v2, and NOT in build_dataset's gated counts. My live-corpus spot-checks confirm the DIRECTION but show several characterizations are loose (yojana is heavily canonical at ~379 early rows; kappavināsa has 3 para-canonical hits, not "absent from the entire canon"; cakkavāḷa has 68 late/para rows, not "almost absent"). The paper presents these uncensused spot-checks beside the rigorously-enumerated person material with the same confident voice and even folds them into "the two dozen or so transitions counted this way," which implies an audit trail they do not have. The biggest concern: a reader cannot tell which half of the paper is enumerated and which is spot-checked, and the cosmology half deserves its own pre-registered census before it carries equal billing in the title. Several internal count discrepancies across the doc set (access-concentration 38/39 vs 135 vs 64; carita 53/30 vs 40/49/59) also need reconciliation.

findings.issue: The entire 'description of the world' domain (cakkavāḷa, kappavināsa, appamāṇa, yojana, nibbāna-as-not-a-place, Mount Meru, world-cycles) is asserted with the same authority as the person-guidance material but is NOT backed by the study's census of record. The HARDENING-CENSUS.md table that produces the '~27 transitions' has rows for jhānaṅga, gotrabhū, ekaggatā, carita, sabhāva, kasiṇa/paṭibhāga-nimitta, ajjhāsaya etc., but NOT cakkavāḷa, kappavināsa, appamāṇa, yojana, or nibbāna. FINDINGS-v2.md (the rigor lane) never mentions cosmology at all. So the world-description half rests on spot-checks, not on the auditable enumeration the abstract promises ('Every count comes from a query grouped on textual stratum against the live corpus').
findings.where: Abstract: 'The description of the world hardens by the same logic. The kasiṇa ... becomes a manufactured disc. The bounded world-sphere (cakkavāḷa) is almost absent from the canon and becomes a commentarial fixture.' and §VII 'Where the hardening happens' whole second paragraph.
findings.recommendation: Either (a) demote the cosmology material to an explicitly-labelled 'spot-check, not censused' status the way the prompt notes it actually is, with a sentence in the method block saying these counts are illustrative not enumerated; or (b) commission a separate pre-registered cosmology census (its own HARDENING-CENSUS rows, sense-audited like the person vocabulary) before the world-description claims carry equal weight with the person-guidance ones. As written the two halves are indistinguishable in confidence.

findings.issue: The title and abstract elevate 'the description of the world' to a co-equal domain ('worked in two registers'), but the rigorous, gated, sense-audited evidence only covers the guidance-of-persons register. The cosmology register is the thinner, spot-checked half. The paper's own conclusion leans on the symmetry ('A present function is set into a fixed essence. An open and measureless formulation is closed into a concrete and bounded one') as if both arms were equally established.
findings.where: Title 'in the guidance of persons and the description of the world'; Conclusion: 'What stays consistently late is one move, worked in two registers.'
findings.recommendation: Rebalance the framing so the cosmology arm is presented as a corroborating illustration of the same shape rather than an independently-established second result, until it has its own census. The 'two registers' symmetry is rhetorically attractive but currently asymmetric in evidentiary support.

findings.issue: 'What was measureless or hyperbolic is later counted in yojanas' implies yojana-measurement is a late/commentarial development. Live count: yojana is heavily CANONICAL. Fine-stratum early-canonical = 379 rows (four Nikayas + Vinaya), late/para = 586, abh = 159, comm = 2764, tika = 5196. The canon already counts distances in yojanas constantly (e.g. travel distances, cosmological scale in DN). The 'later counted in yojanas' claim overstates the lateness of the measurement unit itself; what is late is the systematic yojana-dating of world-cycles, not yojana-counting per se.
findings.where: Abstract: 'What was measureless or hyperbolic is later counted in yojanas and dated by world-cycles.' and §VII: 'is later counted in yojanas and Mount Meru and dated in world-ages.'
findings.recommendation: Narrow the claim to the specific late move (systematic cosmological dimensioning / world-cycle dating) and drop the implication that yojana-measurement as such is post-canonical. Cite a specific late construction rather than the bare unit. 379 canonical yojana rows is fatal to the bare version.

findings.issue: 'The cycle by which a world is destroyed and reformed (kappavināsa) is absent from the entire canon.' Live count: kappavināsa is early-canonical = 0, abh = 0, BUT late/para-canonical = 3 (comm 22, tika 19). Whether those 3 rows are late-canonical Khuddaka or para-canonical (Netti/Peṭakopadesa/Milinda) decides whether 'absent from the entire canon' is exactly true; the paper is elsewhere scrupulous about the late/para distinction, so 'entire canon' is loose here.
findings.where: §VII: 'The cycle by which a world is destroyed and reformed (kappavināsa) is absent from the entire canon and is built in the commentaries.'
findings.recommendation: Change to 'absent from the four Nikāyas and the Abhidhamma' (which is what is verified) or check the 3 late/para rows and qualify accordingly. The compound term may be absent while the concept of world-destruction (saṃvaṭṭa/vivaṭṭa, e.g. DN 27 Aggañña) is present, which is a separate point the paper does not address.

findings.issue: 'The bounded world-sphere (cakkavāḷa), almost absent from the canon, becomes a commentarial fixture at over five hundred occurrences.' Live count: early = 2, late/para = 68, comm = 559, tika = 202. 'over five hundred' (comm 559) is correct, but the 68 late/para hits are non-trivial; 'almost absent from the canon' is fair only if 'canon' excludes the late/para Khuddaka the paper otherwise treats as within-canon strata.
findings.where: Abstract and §VII: 'The bounded world-sphere (cakkavāḷa) is almost absent from the canon and becomes a commentarial fixture.'
findings.recommendation: State 'almost absent from the four Nikāyas (2 rows) and bloom in the commentary (559)', and note the 68 late/para rows so the late-canonical foothold is visible rather than hidden under 'almost absent.'

findings.issue: §VII says 'Of the two dozen or so transitions counted this way, most localize to a single jump.' This maps to HARDENING-CENSUS's '~27 distinct transitions: ~17 support'. But the cosmology transitions named in the surrounding prose (cakkavāḷa, kappavināsa, appamāṇa, yojana, nibbāna-realms) are NOT among the 27 censused rows. So 'counted this way' claims an enumeration that does not include the cosmology items the same paragraph is presenting. The reader is led to believe the world-description claims are part of the same audited count when they are not.
findings.where: §VII: 'Of the two dozen or so transitions counted this way, most localize to a single jump.' juxtaposed with the preceding cosmology paragraph.
findings.recommendation: Either add the cosmology terms as real rows to the HARDENING-CENSUS (with sense-audit) so 'counted this way' is true, or separate the censused person/samādhi transitions from the spot-checked cosmology ones in the prose so the audited '~27' is not silently extended to cover uncensused material.

findings.issue: The access-concentration count is given three different ways across the doc set. Readable §VII: 'access concentration ... found only from the aṭṭhakathā onward (38 rows there, 39 in the Visuddhimagga, against zero in the canon).' HANDOFF-SAMADHI-EXHAUSTIVE: 'upacāra-samādhi 0c/135cm' and 'appanā-samādhi 0c/116'. HARDENING-CENSUS: 'access/absorption tiers (upacāra/appanā-samādhi) ... comm 64, tika 48.' These are plausibly different queries (upacāra-samādhi compound vs upacāra alone vs upacāra+appanā combined vs split attha/vism) but the readable paper does not say which, and 38+39 vs 135 vs 64 will not reconcile for a reader who checks.
findings.where: §VII: 'each zero before' / 'found only from the aṭṭhakathā onward (38 rows there, 39 in the Visuddhimagga, against zero in the canon).'
findings.recommendation: Pin the exact query behind the 38/39 split (presumably upacāra-samādhi as a compound, attha vs vism) and make it consistent with the HANDOFF's verified floor (upacāra-samādhi 0c/135cm). One of the two numbers is for a different string; reconcile and state the stem.

findings.issue: The carita-compound distribution is reported as '53 structurally-mula rows, 30 Visuddhimagga' (readable §V and FINDINGS-v2), but DISCOVERY-PASS §3 reports the same compound as 'canon-mula 0 / attha 40 / vism 49 / tika 59' by work_role, and HARDENING-CENSUS reports 'rāgacarita: late 12, para 9, comm 58, tika 43.' Three different framings (mula-tag with Vism counted as mula; work_role; fine-stratum) yield three different headline numbers for the same compound. A reader reconciling the documents will be confused about whether '30 Vism' and '49 vism' and '58 comm' describe the same rows.
findings.where: §V: 'of the fifty-three structurally-canonical rows that hold the disambiguated temperament compound, thirty are the Visuddhimagga.'
findings.recommendation: Add one clarifying clause that the '53 mula / 30 Vism' count uses the structural mula tag (under which the Visuddhimagga is tagged mula), distinct from the work_role and fine-stratum splits used elsewhere. This is a presentation fix, not a data error, but the three numbers need a reconciling note.

findings.issue: 'the canon defines nibbāna only in the negative, the unmade and the unborn, and declines to call it a place, while the later cosmology fills the afterworld with tiered and measured realms.' This is a substantive doctrinal claim carrying real weight in the cosmology arm, but it is given with NO citation, NO count, and no corpus warrant. There is no nibbāna census anywhere in the study directory (confirmed: the only nibbāna mentions are incidental). The Ud 8.1-8.3 / Iti 43 asaṅkhata material is the obvious warrant and is simply not cited.
findings.where: §VII: 'the canon defines nibbāna only in the negative, the unmade and the unborn, and declines to call it a place.'
findings.recommendation: Either cite the warrant (Ud 8.1 atthi ajātaṃ; Iti 43; the asaṅkhata-saṃyutta SN 43) and/or down-grade to a flagged spot-observation; or cut the sentence. As written it is the one cosmology claim with zero traceable backing in a paper whose method promises every count resolves to a real row.

findings.issue: The 'setup-and-payoff' comma-spliced reversal cadence that EDITOR-CHECKLIST.md names as the operator's signature AI-tell recurs throughout. The checklist even quotes the exact form 'not the absence of person-typing, but the absence of a term' as the canonical bad example, and that construction appears almost verbatim in §III: 'the silence of carita is the absence of a term, not the absence of person-typing.' Other instances: abstract 'It is not a single break ... and it is a tendency rather than a law'; §II 'The pairing is functional, not characterological'; §IV 'It is not a stable canonical meaning the commentary merely tidied up'; the Findings closer 'a found, evidenced pattern, not a conjecture.'
findings.where: §III: 'The lesson is exact and worth stating plainly: the silence of carita is the absence of a term, not the absence of person-typing.'
findings.recommendation: Run the de-AI pass specifically on the antithesis-with-payoff cadence (break each into two flat sentences). Memory note writing-rules-from-ig-notes flags this exact issue; the readable paper is clean on em-dashes/first-person/banned-phrases but still carries the rhythm the operator most dislikes.

findings.issue: The provenance-signature framework (R1-R5, the 11-axis signature) governs FINDINGS-v2 and is the method memory notes say was retrofitted across all 5 studies. The readable paper's method block describes the discovery-pass / sense-audit / function-vs-essence machinery but does not mention the provenance signature at all, even though that framework is what licenses the 'post-canonical, not merely commentarial' stratum claim the readable abstract leans on ('It fixes only in the commentaries'; 'staged rather than single'). A reader moving between the readable and v2 lanes will not see that the chronology-stratum claims come from the signature's stratum axis.
findings.where: Method-in-brief block (lines 17-19) and abstract's stratum language ('across six textual strata').
findings.recommendation: This is acceptable for a readable lane that deliberately strips method, but verify the six-stratum coding in the readable paper matches the v2/signature stratum coding exactly (v2 uses para-canonical-vs-classical-commentary; readable uses a six-stratum ladder including 'late canon' and 'sub-commentary'). The two lanes should not disagree on how many strata there are or where carita 'fixes.'

findings.issue: G2 (the assortative-association / homophily 'law', SN 14.14) is presented as a high-confidence general finding, but it is a single saṃyutta's formula (17 rows) lifted out of context and given a strong sociological gloss ('a canonical sociology of association', 'an explicit homophily principle with a named mechanism'). The dhātu here is being read as 'disposition'; whether dhātu in SN 14 means psychological disposition vs element-in-the-technical-sense is exactly the kind of sense-audit the paper demands of every other term, and it is not shown for G2. This deserves its own treatment before being asserted as a 'law.'
findings.where: Findings of general importance, G2: 'It is an explicit homophily principle with a named mechanism: shared dhātu or disposition. It is asserted as a continuous law of how beings assort.'
findings.recommendation: Flag G2 as a lead for a dedicated study rather than a settled finding, or add the dhātu sense-audit (the SN 14 dhātu-saṃyutta mixes the 18-element technical sense with the disposition sense across its suttas) so the 'disposition' reading is earned. 'Law' is a strong word for one formula.

findings.issue: The abstract and §VII state the hardening 'happens at four separate jumps' and the conclusion calls the systematization 'gradual and many-staged', but the underlying HARDENING-CENSUS verdict is more hedged: 'H1 is substantially supported but not universal, and H0 is partly true ... 63% clean support, 70% if mixed counts.' The readable paper reports the strong version (multi-jump confirmed) and mentions the counter-cases, but does not surface that the pre-committed null (H0: hardening concentrated at one jump) is judged 'partly true' or that support is ~63%. A reader gets 'four jumps, confirmed' more than 'a strong tendency at ~63% with a partly-true null.'
findings.where: §VII: 'The hardening is not one event. It happens at four separate jumps' and Conclusion 'the systematization of the teaching is gradual and many-staged.'
findings.recommendation: Carry the quantified verdict (≈17/27 support, the null partly true) into the readable lane, even in one sentence, so the strength of the multi-jump claim is calibrated to the census rather than stated categorically. The data support a strong tendency, not a clean four-jump law, and the paper's own honesty section would be served by the number.

findings.issue: The footer points the cosmology-relevant reader to DISCOVERY-PASS.md and individual-guidance.json for the temperament census, but neither contains the cosmology or the multi-jump hardening census; the actual backing for §VII's hardening table is HARDENING-CENSUS.md, which the footer does not cite. A reader trying to audit the §VII counts is pointed at the wrong files.
findings.where: Closing footer: 'The wide-recall discovery pass ... are recorded in research/individual-guidance/DISCOVERY-PASS.md.'
findings.recommendation: Add HARDENING-CENSUS.md to the reproducibility pointer as the source for the §VII 'where the hardening happens' counts, and note explicitly that the cosmology figures are spot-checks not in that census.
